random50.title: Priority Seating Respect
random50.summary: Priority seats near train doors are for elderly, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. Offer your seat when needed.

random50.title: Oshibori Etiquette
random50.summary: Use the oshibori (wet towel) only to clean your hands, not your face or neck. Fold it neatly after use and place it back on the tray.

random50.title: Separate Your Trash
random50.summary: Japan has detailed trash separation: burnable, non-burnable, plastic bottles, cans, glass, paper. Follow the labels on bins.

random50.title: Fushimi Inari Best Times
random50.summary: Visit Fushimi Inari at dawn or dusk to avoid crowds. The shrine is open 24 hours. Upper paths are less crowded than the famous lower gates.

random50.title: Miyajima Floating Torii
random50.summary: The torii gate is sacred - don't climb on it or throw coins at it. Best photos at high tide (gate in water) or low tide (walk to gate).

random50.title: Tsukiji Outer Market Etiquette
random50.summary: Don't block vendor stalls while eating. Eat at designated areas or standing by the stall. Don't touch products without purchasing.

random50.title: Otaru Canal District
random50.summary: The historic canal is beautiful but narrow paths get crowded. Visit at dusk when gas lamps light up. Winter illumination is magical but extremely cold.

random50.title: Karaoke Time Limits
random50.summary: Karaoke is charged by time (usually 30-min blocks). Staff will call when time is up - extend or leave promptly. Peak hours have strict limits.

random50.title: Tea Ceremony Participation
random50.summary: Accept the tea bowl with your right hand, place in left palm, rotate clockwise 2-3 times to avoid drinking from the front. Finish in 3 sips.

random50.title: Night Illuminations
random50.summary: Autumn and winter bring spectacular light-up events at temples and gardens. Check schedules - many require advance tickets. Photography is often allowed.

random50.title: TeamLab Booking Essential
random50.summary: TeamLab exhibits require advance tickets - often sold out weeks ahead. Wear comfortable shoes (lots of walking) and clothes you don't mind getting wet.

random50.title: Robot Restaurant Experience
random50.summary: It's loud, flashy, and touristy but uniquely Japanese. Book online for discounts. Don't expect fine dining - it's about the show. Cameras are allowed.

random50.title: Utilize Tokyo Metro Day Passes
random50.summary: For heavy subway use in Tokyo, consider a 24, 48, or 72-hour Tokyo Metro ticket for unlimited rides within a set period.

random50.title: Discover Fukushuen Garden for Free
random50.summary: Enjoy the serene beauty of Fukushuen Garden, a traditional Chinese landscape garden in Naha, offering a peaceful escape at no cost.

random50.title: Solo Photography
random50.summary: Traveling solo means freedom to wait for the perfect shot. Dawn at temples, blue hour in cities — no one's waiting on you. A tripod and patience replace the need for a travel companion.

random50.title: Teeth Sucking Sound
random50.summary: When Japanese people suck air through their teeth (sss sound), it signals hesitation, difficulty, or that they're thinking of how to politely refuse.

random50.title: Understand Omotenashi Service
random50.summary: Appreciate "omotenashi," the unique Japanese hospitality characterized by anticipating needs without expectation of reward, evident in Tokyo's service.

random50.title: Maintain Quiet Reflection
random50.summary: Speak softly and keep phones silent on temple grounds to preserve the peaceful atmosphere.

random50.title: Winter Snow Delays
random50.summary: Heavy snowfall in northern Japan and along the Sea of Japan coast can delay or cancel trains. The Shinkansen is surprisingly resilient.

random50.title: Arima Onsen Tattoo Rules
random50.summary: Before visiting Arima Onsen, check individual bathhouse (onsen) policies regarding tattoos, as some may still restrict entry or require covers.

random50.title: Capture Kobe's Million Dollar Night View
random50.summary: Head to Kikuseidai viewpoint on Mount Maya for breathtaking panoramic night views of Kobe, Osaka, and Wakayama, often called the "Million Dollar View."

random50.title: Enjoying Authentic Kobe Beef
random50.summary: Savor Kobe Beef at a specialty restaurant. Appreciate the marbling and preparation; typically, no need for extra condiments beyond a dash of salt.

random50.title: Meriken Park Photo Opportunities
random50.summary: Capture iconic shots at Meriken Park, featuring the Kobe Port Tower, Maritime Museum, and unique art installations, especially beautiful at sunset.

random50.title: Reflect at the Earthquake Memorial
random50.summary: Visit the Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake Memorial Park to understand the devastation of the 1995 earthquake and Kobe's incredible recovery.

random50.title: Savor Uji Matcha Delicacies
random50.summary: Indulge in Uji matcha, a premium green tea from Kyoto, found in various sweets, drinks, and traditional tea ceremonies throughout the city.

random50.title: Understand Machiya Architecture
random50.summary: Appreciate Kyoto's traditional wooden townhouses (machiya), noting their narrow facades and deep interiors, reflecting historical tax laws.

random50.title: Enjoy Osaka's Winter Illuminations
random50.summary: Experience the magical winter illuminations across Osaka from late November to December, with major displays in Umeda and Nakanoshima.

random50.title: Respect Exhibit Photography Rules
random50.summary: Always check for "no photography" signs, especially near artifacts or in specific halls, as rules vary greatly between museums.

random50.title: Book Popular Museum Tickets Ahead
random50.summary: Purchase tickets in advance for highly popular museums like Ghibli Museum or TeamLab Borderless, often weeks or months ahead of your visit.

random50.title: Navigate Tatami Seating
random50.summary: Tatami mat seating (zashiki) in traditional restaurants may be challenging; inquire about Western-style tables.

random50.title: Photograph Dazaifu Tenmangu Respectfully
random50.summary: Capture the beauty of Dazaifu Tenmangu Shrine's grounds and main hall exterior, but refrain from photography during active rituals or inside sacred inner areas.

random50.title: Respect Atsuta Jingu Shrine
random50.summary: When visiting Atsuta Jingu, a major shrine, follow the general shrine etiquette of bowing at the torii and purifying at the temizuya.

random50.title: Yokocho Alley Culture
random50.summary: Narrow yokocho alleys with tiny bars seating 6–8 people are Japan's social nightlife. One or two drinks per spot, then move on. Bartenders or regulars may wave you in — that's an invitation.

random50.title: Understand Botchan's Legacy
random50.summary: Appreciate the deep cultural impact of Natsume Soseki's novel 'Botchan' on Matsuyama's identity and various local attractions.

random50.title: Greet Elders with Respect
random50.summary: In Okinawa, showing respect to elders (Uchinaanchu) is deeply valued. A polite bow and soft tone are always appreciated.

random50.title: Discover Date Masamune's Legacy
random50.summary: Learn about Date Masamune, the "One-Eyed Dragon," who founded Sendai. His influence is visible at Sendai Castle Ruins and his ornate Zuihoden Mausoleum.

random50.title: Hiking Solo in Japan
random50.summary: Popular trails are well-marked and busy enough that you're never truly alone. Carry water and check last bus times from trailheads — some rural buses stop running by 5 PM.

random50.title: Rural Buses: Board Rear, Pay Front, Carry Coins
random50.summary: Outside cities, board from the rear door and take a numbered ticket. Pay the exact fare at the front when exiting. Many rural buses accept only cash — carry coins.

random50.title: New Cycling Fines from April 2026
random50.summary: Japan is enforcing ¥5,000-9,000 fines for bicycle traffic violations — including tourists. Ride on the left side of the road, not sidewalks. Use bike lights after dark.

random50.title: Retro Game Arcades Are Living Museums
random50.summary: Akihabara's Super Potato, Nakano Broadway, and Osaka's Den Den Town have retro game floors with playable cabinets from the 1980s for ¥100 per play.

random50.title: Egg Is Everywhere — Tamago Alert
random50.summary: Egg (tamago/卵) appears in unexpected places: ramen toppings, tonkatsu batter, okonomiyaki, custard-filled bread, mayo on everything. Ask "tamago nashi" for no egg.

random50.title: Hot Canned Coffee from Vending Machines
random50.summary: In winter, vending machines sell hot canned coffee (marked with a red label). Boss and Georgia are the main brands. A hot can costs ¥120-150 and warms your hands too.

random50.title: Tokyo Taiyaki — Fish-Shaped Sweet Cakes
random50.summary: Taiyaki (鯛焼き) are fish-shaped cakes filled with sweet red bean paste (anko). Classic versions cost ¥200-300. Look for shops that make them one at a time (ippon-yaki).

random50.title: Call the Staff with Sumimasen
random50.summary: Raise your hand and say "sumimasen" (すみません) to call your server. Many izakayas have a call button on the table. Servers won't check on you unprompted.

random50.title: Don't Walk and Eat — Stand Near the Stall
random50.summary: Walking while eating (tabearuki) is considered rude in Japan. Buy your food, eat it near the vendor, and dispose of trash there before moving on.

random50.title: Hakata Tonkotsu — Fukuoka's Creamy Signature
random50.summary: Fukuoka's Hakata ramen features a rich, milky pork bone broth (tonkotsu) with thin straight noodles. Order firm noodles (katame) for the local experience.

random50.title: Hiroshima Okonomiyaki Is Layered, Not Mixed
random50.summary: Unlike Osaka's mixed-batter style, Hiroshima-style layers noodles, cabbage, bean sprouts, pork, and egg in distinct strata. They're fundamentally different dishes. Try both.

random50.title: Nightlife Cover Charges
random50.summary: Clubs charge entry (¥1,000–3,000) often including one drink. Some require ID — carry your passport or a photo of it. Dress codes are relaxed by Western standards but avoid flip-flops and tank tops.

random50.title: Beat the Heat: Morning Temple Strategy
random50.summary: Start sightseeing by 7-8am when temples open and temps are 24-26°C. By noon it hits 32°C+. Reserve afternoons for indoor museums, malls, or rest.

random50.title: Family-Friendly Neighborhoods to Stay
random50.summary: Ueno (parks, zoo, museums), Odaiba (beaches, teamLab, malls), Namba (street food, arcades), Arashiyama (monkeys, bamboo, trains) — neighborhoods where families thrive without long commutes.

highPriority.title: Peak Sakura in Tokyo and Kansai
highPriority.summary: Full bloom (mankai) hits Tokyo around March 28-April 2, Kyoto/Osaka April 2-8. Petals scatter within a week. Check daily forecasts to time your visit.

highPriority.title: Observe Quiet at Peace Park
highPriority.summary: Maintain a solemn and respectful demeanor within Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park and Museum. This is a site of deep historical significance.

highPriority.title: Vegetarian Dining Is Tricky — Dashi Is Everywhere
highPriority.summary: Most Japanese 'vegetable' dishes use dashi stock made from bonito (fish flakes). Even miso soup, simmered vegetables, and rice seasonings often contain fish-based dashi.

highPriority.title: How to Communicate Allergies in Japanese
highPriority.summary: Say "[ingredient] arerugii ga arimasu" (I have a [__] allergy). Key terms: tamago (egg), ebi (shrimp), komugi (wheat), gyunyu (milk), soba (buckwheat), kani (crab).

highPriority.title: Golden Week Crowds: April 29 Through May 5
highPriority.summary: Golden Week is Japan's biggest holiday stretch. Expect sold-out shinkansen, 2-3x hotel prices, and 60+ minute waits at popular attractions.

highPriority.title: Golden Week Begins April 29
highPriority.summary: Golden Week starts with Showa Day (Apr 29). Shinkansen, hotels, and flights book solid 2-3 months ahead. Prices surge 50-100% from April 28 through May 5.

highPriority.title: Peak Summer Heat: 33-38°C Daily
highPriority.summary: August is Japan's hottest month. Tokyo and Osaka regularly hit 35°C+ with oppressive humidity. Limit outdoor exposure between 11am-3pm.

highPriority.title: Hydration Is Non-Negotiable
highPriority.summary: Carry water constantly. Vending machines every 200 meters sell cold drinks for ¥110-160. Look for Pocari Sweat or Aquarius for electrolyte replacement.

highPriority.title: New Year Rush Starts December 28
highPriority.summary: Most businesses close December 29-January 3 for oshogatsu (New Year). Department stores, banks, and museums shut down. Konbini and some restaurants stay open.

highPriority.title: Booking Accessible JR Pass Seats
highPriority.summary: Reserve wheelchair-accessible shinkansen seats at Midori no Madoguchi at least 2 days before travel. Online booking doesn't support accessible seat selection yet.

highPriority.title: Solo Dining Is Normal in Japan
highPriority.summary: Counter seats (kauntā/カウンター) at ramen shops, sushi bars, and izakaya are designed for solo diners. You'll never get a strange look — solo dining is mainstream culture here.

highPriority.title: Emergency Contacts and Solo Safety Net
highPriority.summary: Police: 110, Ambulance: 119, JNTO Tourist Helpline: 050-3816-2787 (English 24/7). Download the Safety Tips app for multilingual disaster alerts. Keep embassy numbers in your phone.

highPriority.title: Pseudoephedrine (Sudafed, Vicks Inhaler) Is BANNED in Japan
highPriority.summary: Pseudoephedrine is classified as a stimulant raw material. Possession can lead to arrest. Leave Sudafed, Actifed, and Vicks nasal inhalers at home — buy Japanese cold medicine instead.

highPriority.title: Prescription Medications Need Advance Documentation
highPriority.summary: ADHD drugs, strong painkillers, and certain anxiety medications require an import certificate from Japan's Ministry of Health — apply at least 2 weeks before travel.

highPriority.title: Understand A-Bomb Dome Symbolism
highPriority.summary: Reflect on the Atomic Bomb Dome's role as a powerful symbol of peace and the devastation of nuclear warfare, preserved as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

highPriority.title: IC Card Basics
highPriority.summary: Suica, PASMO, and Icoca are interchangeable across Japan. Tap in and out at ticket gates. Works on trains, buses, and at convenience stores.

highPriority.title: Last Train Times and Planning Around Them
highPriority.summary: Last trains run roughly midnight-12:30am in most cities. Miss it and you're looking at ¥5,000-15,000 taxi fares or a capsule hotel. Check your exact last train on the Navitime app.

highPriority.title: Peak Koyo in Kyoto and Tokyo: Mid-to-Late November
highPriority.summary: Kyoto's maples peak November 15-30. Tokyo's Meiji Jingu Gaien ginkgo avenue and Rikugien Garden peak around November 20-December 5.

highPriority.title: Wash Before Onsen
highPriority.summary: Always shower and wash thoroughly at the cleaning stations before entering the onsen bath. The bath is for soaking, not cleaning.

highPriority.title: Respect Geiko Privacy
highPriority.summary: In Gion, do not approach, touch, or block geiko/maiko. Taking photos without permission is intrusive. Observe respectfully from a distance.

highPriority.title: Purify at Temizuya
highPriority.summary: Cleanse your hands and mouth at the water basin before entering shrine grounds. Left hand first, then right, then rinse mouth (without touching ladle to lips).

highPriority.title: Earthquake Safety
highPriority.summary: Drop, cover, and hold on during earthquakes. Move away from windows and shelves. If outdoors, stay away from buildings and power lines.

highPriority.title: Geisha District Manners
highPriority.summary: In geisha districts (Gion, Pontocho), don't chase, touch, or block geiko/maiko. Private streets exist - respect 'no entry' signs.

highPriority.title: Use the Meipuru~pu Bus
highPriority.summary: Navigate Hiroshima's main attractions efficiently using the "Meipuru~pu" (Maple Loop) sightseeing bus, included with the JR Pass.

highPriority.title: Capture Miyajima Torii at Tide
highPriority.summary: Photograph the iconic "floating" Itsukushima Shrine torii gate during high tide for the best reflective shots, ideally at dawn or dusk for dramatic lighting.

highPriority.title: No Flash at Peace Museum
highPriority.summary: Respect photography restrictions within the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, especially avoiding flash photography to preserve artifacts and show reverence.

highPriority.title: Learn About Senbazuru
highPriority.summary: Discover the story of Sadako Sasaki and the "senbazuru" (thousand paper cranes) at the Children's Peace Monument, symbolizing hope and peace.

highPriority.title: Savor Hiroshima Okonomiyaki
highPriority.summary: Try Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, a layered savory pancake featuring noodles (soba or udon), cabbage, and often pork or seafood.

highPriority.title: Respect Dogo Onsen Honkan Queue
highPriority.summary: Be mindful of queues and follow staff guidance when choosing your bathing course at the historic Dogo Onsen Honkan, especially during busy periods.

highPriority.title: Savor Matsuyama Tai-meshi
highPriority.summary: Indulge in Matsuyama's renowned sea bream rice (Tai-meshi), a local specialty served either mixed or with sashimi, a true culinary experience.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Priority Seating Respect
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Priority seats near train doors are for elderly, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. Offer your seat when needed.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Summer Heat on Platforms
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Open-air platforms get very hot in summer (July-September). Underground stations and air-conditioned cars are a relief. Stay hydrated.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Bowing Basics
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: A slight bow (15°) is appropriate for casual greetings. Deeper bows show more respect. As a tourist, a small nod or bow is appreciated.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Queue Lines on Platforms
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Line up at marked waiting areas on train platforms. Let passengers exit before boarding. Don't rush or push.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Separate Your Trash
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan has detailed trash separation: burnable, non-burnable, plastic bottles, cans, glass, paper. Follow the labels on bins.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Point with Open Hand
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Pointing with a finger is considered rude. Use an open hand with palm facing up to indicate direction or items.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Quiet on Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Keep phone conversations and loud chatter to a minimum on trains. Set phones to silent/manner mode. Shinkansen has designated quiet cars.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Use Both Hands for Exchanges
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Present business cards, gifts, and money with both hands. Receive items the same way. Shows respect and gratitude.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: IC Card Convenience
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Get a Suica or Pasmo card for seamless travel. Works on trains, buses, and even convenience stores. Saves time and avoids ticket hassles.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Earthquake Safety
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Drop, cover, and hold on during earthquakes. Move away from windows and shelves. If outdoors, stay away from buildings and power lines.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Smoking Restrictions
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Smoking while walking is prohibited in most areas. Use designated smoking rooms or outdoor smoking areas. Fines apply for violations.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Queue Jumping is Taboo
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Never cut in line. Japanese queuing culture is serious - people queue quietly and patiently, even for hours. Respect the order.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Train Travel with Kids
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Children under 6 ride free on trains. Ages 6–11 are half price. Reserve seats on shinkansen — the last car has extra legroom and is closest to the multi-purpose room for diaper changes.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Obon Travel Warning
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Obon (mid-August) is peak domestic travel. Trains, flights, and hotels book up fast. Expect huge crowds at popular destinations. Book months ahead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Solo Photography
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Traveling solo means freedom to wait for the perfect shot. Dawn at temples, blue hour in cities — no one's waiting on you. A tripod and patience replace the need for a travel companion.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: The X Gesture
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Crossed arms or fingers making an X means 'no,' 'not available,' or 'closed.' Staff use this when they can't accommodate your request.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Teeth Sucking Sound
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: When Japanese people suck air through their teeth (sss sound), it signals hesitation, difficulty, or that they're thinking of how to politely refuse.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Free Tissue Packets
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: People handing out tissue packets on the street are advertising. Take them freely - it's normal and the tissues are useful (public restrooms may lack paper).

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Last Train Times
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Last trains (終電 shuden) run around 11:30 PM - 12:30 AM depending on the line. Miss it and you're stuck until 5 AM. Check times for YOUR station, not terminus.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Heated Toilet Seats
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese toilets have heated seats, bidets, and sound effects (to mask noise). Buttons are usually labeled in English. The big button is always flush.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Airport Luggage Delivery
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Send luggage directly to/from airports with Yamato or Sagawa. Drop off 2 days before flight. Pick up at airport counter. No dragging bags on trains!

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Love Hotels Aren't Shady
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Love hotels offer clean, themed rooms with great facilities. 'Rest' (2-4 hours) or 'Stay' (overnight) rates. Often cheaper than business hotels. Anyone can use them.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Garbage Day Confusion
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Residential areas have strict garbage collection schedules - different days for burnable, plastic, glass, etc. If staying in an Airbnb, ask your host about the rules.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Night Buses Save Money
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Overnight highway buses between cities cost 1/3 of shinkansen fares. Not glamorous but practical. Book through Willer Express or Japan Bus Lines.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: The Polite Refusal
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: If you hear 'muzukashii desu ne' (that's difficult) or 'chotto...' with a pained expression, it's a no. Japanese avoid direct refusals. Accept gracefully.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Apologies Don't Mean Guilt
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese people apologize frequently even when not at fault - 'sumimasen' is used for thanks, excuse me, and sorry. It maintains social harmony.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Convenience Store Supplies
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Conbini carry diapers, baby wipes, kids' drinks, and simple snacks. In a pinch, they're more reliable than pharmacies for quick resupply — and they're on every corner.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Green Car Upgrade
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: JR Green Cars (グリーン車) are first class — wider seats, more legroom, quieter. Worth the upgrade on long Shinkansen rides. Book at ticket office.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Suica Refund Before Leaving
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Return your IC card at any JR ticket office before leaving Japan. You'll get back the ¥500 deposit plus remaining balance (minus ¥220 fee).

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Enjoy Post-New Year Calm
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: After the initial New Year's rush (early Jan), major tourist spots and transport become significantly less crowded, offering a more relaxed sightseeing experience.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Find Winter Travel Deals
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Mid-to-late January often presents opportunities for cheaper flights and accommodation as demand drops after the New Year holiday period, especially for domestic travel.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Conserve Energy Indoors
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: While buildings are well-heated, be mindful of excessive energy use. Turn off lights and AC when leaving rooms, and consider wearing an extra layer instead of high heating.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Be Mindful of Indoor Heating
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: While it's cold outside, indoor heating can be intense. Adjust room temperatures responsibly and turn off lights/heating when leaving your accommodation to save energy.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Check Winter Transport Advisories
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Monitor weather forecasts for potential train (especially Shinkansen) and flight delays, particularly if traveling through snowy regions like Hokkaido, Tohoku, or the Japan Alps.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Solo Safety in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan is one of the safest countries for solo travelers. Trains run late, streets are well-lit, and convenience stores are 24/7. Trust your instincts but know the baseline risk is very low.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Solo Travel by Train
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Train travel in Japan is designed for individuals. Reserved seats mean guaranteed space. Platform markings show exactly where to stand. Announcements are in English on major lines.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Pacing for Families
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Plan 2–3 activities per day maximum with kids. Build in konbini stops and park breaks. After lunch is usually nap or quiet time — schedule indoor or low-key activities then.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Toilets
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Multi-purpose toilets (tahyō toire) are in most train stations, department stores, and public facilities. They're spacious with grab bars, changing tables, and ostomate support. Look for the wheelc...

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Capsule Hotels
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Capsule hotels are designed for solo travelers. Modern ones have clean pods, shared baths, and secure lockers. They're budget-friendly and a uniquely Japanese experience worth trying at least once.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: IC Cards Cannot Cross Regional Boundaries
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: You can't tap in at Tokyo and tap out at Osaka — IC cards only work within a single regional zone. Exit the system, buy a long-distance ticket, then use your card again for local transit at your destination.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: IC Card Refunds Are Region-Locked
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Suica cards can only be refunded at JR East stations, ICOCA only at JR West. If you buy ICOCA in Osaka and try to return it in Tokyo, you can't get the ¥500 deposit back.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Welcome Suica App for iPhone
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: The Welcome Suica Mobile App lets iPhone users set up a digital Suica before landing in Japan. No physical card needed, no airport queue — activate it on the plane.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Nozomi Goes All-Reserved During Peak Holidays
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: During Golden Week, Obon, and New Year, all Nozomi shinkansen seats become reserved-only. No unreserved cars. Book early or switch to Hikari/Kodama which still have free seating.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Board Unreserved Shinkansen from the Origin Station
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: For unreserved seats, board where the train starts empty — Tokyo, Shin-Osaka, or Hakata. Boarding midway at Shinagawa or Kyoto often means standing the entire ride.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: JR Pass Now Supports QR Code Gate Entry
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: JR Pass holders can book seats via the SmartEX app and use QR codes at selected ticket gates. No more queuing at counter windows for every reservation.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Takkyubin Cannot Deliver to Most Airbnbs
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Luggage forwarding requires a staffed reception desk. Most Airbnbs and vacation rentals can't receive packages. Hotels, ryokans, and serviced apartments work fine.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Send Luggage to the Airport 2+ Days Early
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Airport luggage delivery takes at least two days. Drop off bags at your hotel or a konbini two days before your flight and travel to the airport hands-free.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Mask Wearing
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many Japanese wear masks when sick, during allergy season, or for privacy. Having a mask is useful and won't attract stares.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: New Cycling Fines from April 2026
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan is enforcing ¥5,000-9,000 fines for bicycle traffic violations — including tourists. Ride on the left side of the road, not sidewalks. Use bike lights after dark.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Reserve Coin Lockers with the SPACER App
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Large lockers vanish by late morning at major stations during peak seasons. The SPACER app shows real-time availability and lets you reserve a locker before arriving.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Locker Fees Reset at Midnight
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Locker charges are per calendar day, not per 24 hours. Storing bags at 11 PM and retrieving at 1 AM costs two days of rental. Maximum storage is 3 days.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Overnight Buses Save a Hotel Night and Transit Cost
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Overnight highway buses run Tokyo-Osaka from ¥3,000 and Tokyo-Kyoto from ¥2,500. Willer Express and JR Bus offer reclining seats and even flat-bed pods. Book early for best prices.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Thank the Bus Driver When You Exit
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Saying "arigatou gozaimashita" to the driver when stepping off the bus is a deeply rooted custom, especially in rural areas. A simple nod works too.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Rainy Season Preparation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tsuyu (rainy season) runs mid-June to mid-July. Pack waterproof bags, quick-dry clothes, and a good umbrella. Indoor backup plans are essential.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: New Year Closures
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many businesses close Dec 31 - Jan 3. Restaurants, shops, and attractions have reduced hours. Plan accordingly and check specific venue schedules.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Train Travel
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: JR stations have platform elevators and wheelchair spaces on trains. Station staff assist with ramps at the gap between platform and train. Request help at the ticket gate — they'll radio ahead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Kairo Heat Packs
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: In winter, buy kairo (カイロ) disposable heat packs at convenience stores. They last 12+ hours. Stick-on versions go inside clothes. Locals swear by them.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Tactile Paving Navigation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Yellow tactile paving lines guide visually impaired travelers through stations and sidewalks. Raised dots mean stop, raised bars mean follow. These are everywhere in urban Japan.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Emergency Phrases
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tasukete (help!), kyūkyūsha (ambulance), keisatsu (police). Dial 119 for fire/ambulance, 110 for police. Japan Helpline 0570-000-911 has 24/7 multilingual support.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Accommodation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Larger hotels have accessible rooms with roll-in showers and lowered fixtures. Traditional ryokan are harder — futons on tatami require floor-level transfers. Book accessible rooms well in advance.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Dress in Warm Layers
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: January in Japan brings cold temperatures; pack thermal underwear, thick coats, hats, and gloves, especially if visiting northern regions or mountains.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Post-Golden Week Deals: May 7 Onward
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Hotel and flight prices drop sharply after May 6. The week of May 7-14 is one of Japan's best-value travel windows with perfect weather and thin crowds.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: May Weather: T-Shirt Days Arrive
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka hit 22-26°C in May with low humidity. The most comfortable month for walking. UV is strong, so pack sunscreen and a hat for temple circuits.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: October Weather: Japan's Best Month
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: October offers 18-24°C daytime temps, low humidity, clear skies, and minimal rain. Pack light layers and one warmer piece for evenings.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Golden Week Crowds: April 29 Through May 5
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Golden Week is Japan's biggest holiday stretch. Expect sold-out shinkansen, 2-3x hotel prices, and 60+ minute waits at popular attractions.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Tsuyu Ends Late July
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Rainy season typically ends in Kanto/Kansai around July 19-21. The transition to full summer is abrupt: expect 30-33°C and high humidity within days.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Pack for Cold, Dry Winter Air
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Bring warm layers, including thermal underwear, waterproof outer shell, gloves, scarves, and warm hats. February can be very cold and dry, especially in northern regions.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Golden Week Begins April 29
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Golden Week starts with Showa Day (Apr 29). Shinkansen, hotels, and flights book solid 2-3 months ahead. Prices surge 50-100% from April 28 through May 5.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Typhoon Season Delays
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: September-October typhoons can halt trains for hours. Check weather forecasts and have backup plans. JR East app shows real-time disruptions.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Peak Summer Heat: 33-38°C Daily
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: August is Japan's hottest month. Tokyo and Osaka regularly hit 35°C+ with oppressive humidity. Limit outdoor exposure between 11am-3pm.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: 'Sumimasen' Is the Most Versatile Word You'll Learn
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: It means 'excuse me' (getting attention), 'I'm sorry' (apologizing), and 'thank you' (expressing gratitude). Use it constantly — entering restaurants, getting help, squeezing past people.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Mold and Humidity: Protect Your Gear
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: June humidity causes camera lens fog when moving between AC and outdoors. Keep silica gel packets in your camera bag and wipe lenses before shooting.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Hydration Is Non-Negotiable
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Carry water constantly. Vending machines every 200 meters sell cold drinks for ¥110-160. Look for Pocari Sweat or Aquarius for electrolyte replacement.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: November Weather: Comfortable but Cooling
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka: 12-18°C daytime, 6-10°C evenings. Kyoto's temples in mountain areas drop colder. Pack a proper jacket and scarf for evening koyo viewing.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Fall Foliage Routes
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Trains to Nikko, Hakone, and Kyoto get crowded during peak koyo (Nov). The Sagano Romantic Train and Hakone Tozan Railway offer scenic autumn views.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Typhoon Awareness Begins
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Typhoon season ramps up in August. Track storms on weather apps 3-5 days out. Flights and ferries cancel; shinkansen may delay. Have flexible backup plans.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Taxi Doors Open Automatically — Don't Touch Them
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese taxi rear doors are controlled by the driver. Don't open or close them yourself. Say '[destination] made onegaishimasu' or show the address on your phone.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Don't Block Doors
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Move to the center of the car if you're not getting off soon. Blocking doors slows boarding and is a pet peeve of commuters.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Heat Lingers Through Mid-September
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Early September still hits 30-33°C in Tokyo and Osaka. The summer heat doesn't truly break until the autumn equinox around September 22-23.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Typhoon Season Peaks in September
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: September is Japan's most active typhoon month. 2-3 typhoons typically approach or make landfall. Monitor forecasts daily and keep plans flexible.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: September Hotel Deals Continue
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: September remains low season for inbound tourism. Hotel prices stay 20-30% below peak across most cities. Typhoon risk keeps crowds down.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Carry a Small Bag for Your Trash at All Times
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan has almost no public trash cans — removed after the 1995 sarin attack. Konbini bins are for in-store purchases only. You'll carry trash back to your hotel.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: December Hotel Pricing: Mixed Bag
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Early-to-mid December offers good hotel value (low season). Prices spike December 23-January 3. Christmas Eve in Tokyo is especially expensive at upscale hotels.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: December Weather: Cold but Clear
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka: 5-12°C daytime, near freezing at night. Kyoto drops to 2-8°C. Hokkaido is fully snowy (-5 to 2°C). Pack proper winter gear.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: New Year Rush Starts December 28
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most businesses close December 29-January 3 for oshogatsu (New Year). Department stores, banks, and museums shut down. Konbini and some restaurants stay open.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Wheelchair Rental at Airports and Major Stations
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Free wheelchair loans available at Narita, Haneda, Kansai, and all major JR stations. Ask at the service counter (midori no madoguchi) — no reservation needed for short-term use.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Separate Your Trash into 3+ Categories
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Even in hotels, you may need to sort: burnables, non-burnables, recyclables (PET bottles, cans). PET bottle caps and labels go in separate bins from the bottle itself.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Sports Day Holiday (Second Monday of October)
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Sports Day (Supootsu no Hi) creates a 3-day weekend. Domestic travel increases but it's not as severe as Golden Week. Book shinkansen reserved seats ahead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Booking Accessible JR Pass Seats
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Reserve wheelchair-accessible shinkansen seats at Midori no Madoguchi at least 2 days before travel. Online booking doesn't support accessible seat selection yet.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Labor Thanksgiving Day: November 23
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Kinro Kansha no Hi (Labor Thanksgiving Day) is a national holiday. Combined with the preceding weekend, it creates a busy 3-day stretch. Book ahead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Japan Rail Pass Value Calculation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: A 7-day JR Pass costs ¥50,000. Tokyo→Kyoto round trip alone is ¥27,320. Add one more long-distance trip and the pass pays for itself. Use the JR Pass calculator online.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Queue on Platform Markings
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Line up at the marked spots on the platform — train doors align precisely. Two lines per door. Let passengers off before boarding.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Stroller-Friendly Trains and Priority Spaces
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most JR and metro trains have wheelchair/stroller spaces near doors. Shinkansen cars 11-12 have extra legroom rows. Elevators exist at all major stations — follow the エレベーター signs.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Service Animal Policies in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Service dogs (hojoken/補助犬) are legally allowed in all public facilities, trains, restaurants, and hotels under the Assistance Dog Act. Carry documentation.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Hotel Booking Sites
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Booking.com and Jalan.net filter for accessible rooms (barrier-free/バリアフリー). Look for roll-in showers, grab bars, and lowered sinks. Book early — stock is limited.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Ostomate-Accessible Restrooms
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Ostomate facilities (オストメイト) with specialized sinks are in most major stations, department stores, and service areas. Look for the ostomate pictogram on restroom signs.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Braille on Vending Machines and Public Signage
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most vending machines have Braille labels on buttons. Train ticket machines have Braille on keypads. Tactile paving (tenshoku burokku) guides to platforms.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Priority Seats on Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Priority seats (yūsen zaseki/優先座席) are marked in a different color near train doors. They're for elderly, disabled, pregnant, and injured passengers.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Reading Slope and Ramp Signage
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Look for スロープ (surōpu/slope), エレベーター (erebētā/elevator), and 段差なし (dansa nashi/no steps). These kanji appear on barrier-free route signs at stations.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Station Elevator Navigation Apps
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Download the JR East or Navitime app for step-free route planning. They show elevator locations, accessible gates, and platform gap sizes at each station.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Disability Discount with Disability Certificate
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: A physical disability certificate (shintai shōgaisha techō/身体障害者手帳) gets you 50% off JR fares over 100km, free companion tickets, and attraction discounts.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Women-Only Cars
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Some trains have women-only cars during rush hour (marked in pink). Men should avoid these cars during designated hours, typically weekday mornings.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Capsule Hotels for Budget Stays
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Capsule hotels cost ¥3,000-4,500/night with shared bathrooms, often including a sauna or public bath. Modern ones (Nine Hours, First Cabin) are clean and design-forward.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Laundry Saves Packing
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Coin laundry (koin randorī) costs ¥200-400 per wash and ¥100 per 10 minutes of drying. Found everywhere — Google Maps 'コインランドリー' to find the nearest one. Pack light and wash often.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Highway Buses vs Shinkansen
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Willer Express and other highway buses run Tokyo→Osaka from ¥3,500 (vs ¥13,870 shinkansen). Night buses save a hotel night. Book on willerexpress.com or kosokubus.com.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Lockers at Stations
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー) in 3 sizes: small (¥400), medium (¥500), large (¥700). Pay with IC card or coins. 3-day max.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Capsule Hotel as Last-Train Backup
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Miss the last train? Walk-in capsule hotels near entertainment districts cost ¥3,000-4,500. No reservation needed. Many include a bath. Jalan.net shows same-night availability.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Safety at Night in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan is among the safest countries for nightlife. Violent crime is extremely rare. Standard precautions apply: avoid touts in Kabukichō/Roppongi, don't accept free drinks from strangers, watch you...

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Late-Night Taxi Premium
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Taxis add a 20% late-night surcharge (深夜割増) between 10pm-5am in most cities. A ¥2,000 daytime ride becomes ¥2,400. Ride-sharing apps (GO, S.RIDE) don't reduce the surcharge.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Drone Regulations in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Drones over 100g require registration with MLIT. No-fly zones include all airports, dense residential areas, and above 150m. Violations carry fines up to ¥500,000.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Backpack Etiquette
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: On crowded trains, hold your backpack in front of you or put it on the luggage rack. Wearing it on your back takes up space and bumps people.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Umbrellas as Photography Props in Rain
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Rainy days create stunning reflections on wet streets and diffused light. A transparent vinyl umbrella (¥500 at any konbini) makes a beautiful photo prop against neon or temple backdrops.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Stand on the Correct Side
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: On escalators, stand on the left in Tokyo (walk on right) and stand on the right in Osaka (walk on left). Follow what locals do.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Keep Conversations Quiet
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Loud conversations on trains are uncommon. Keep your voice low, especially on morning commutes. Headphones should not leak sound.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: January-February Is the Best Value Season in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Lowest accommodation prices, fewest crowds, excellent winter seafood (crab, oyster, fugu). Combine with Sapporo Snow Festival, onsen trips, and winter illuminations.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Avoid Major Cities During Golden Week (April 29-May 6)
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: This cluster of national holidays creates Japan's worst tourism crush. Hotels triple in price, shinkansen sell out, temples queue for hours. Head to less-visited cities instead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Emergency Number: 110/119
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Police: 110, Fire/Ambulance: 119. English support available but limited. Keep your accommodation address handy to provide location.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Pocket WiFi and eSIM Options
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Pocket WiFi rentals (¥500-900/day) ship to airports or hotels. eSIMs from Ubigi, Airalo, or IIJmio activate instantly from ¥500 for 3 days. Essential for solo navigation with Google Maps.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Peak Typhoon Danger Is Late August Through September
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Typhoons can halt trains, close attractions, and ground flights. Okinawa and Kyushu get hit worst. Check the JMA forecast 3-5 days ahead and have backup plans ready.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Japan Is 43% Cashless — But You Still Need Cash
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Credit cards work at chains and department stores, but small ramen shops, izakayas, street food stalls, rural ryokans, and shrine admission often require cash. Carry ¥10,000-20,000.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Carry Your Trash
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Public trash cans are rare in Japan. Carry a small bag for your trash until you find a bin at a convenience store or train station.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Shinkansen Luggage Rules
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Oversized bags (over 160cm total dimensions) need a free seat reservation for the last-row luggage space. Book when buying your ticket.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Drone Regulations
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Drone flying is heavily restricted in Japan. Prohibited near airports, crowds, and most tourist sites. Permits required for many areas.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Backpack Etiquette on Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Remove backpacks and hold them in front of you or place them on overhead racks. Large bags take up space and bump other passengers.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Hotels Hold Luggage
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most hotels will hold your luggage before check-in and after check-out for free. Drop bags in the morning and explore unencumbered.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Golden Week Chaos
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Golden Week (late April - early May) is Japan's longest holiday. Everything is crowded and expensive. Book 3+ months ahead or avoid entirely.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Cash is Still King
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many small shops, restaurants, and traditional businesses are cash-only. Always carry ¥10,000-20,000 in cash. ATMs at convenience stores accept foreign cards.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Shinkansen Seat Rotation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Shinkansen seats can rotate 180° to face your group. Press the pedal at the base and push the back. Rotate them back when you leave.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Rush Hour Survival Tips
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: During rush hour: hold belongings close, avoid large luggage, face the same direction as everyone, and breathe. Platform staff may push you in.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Credit Card PINs Now Required — No More Signatures
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Since April 2025, IC chip credit card transactions require your 4-digit PIN. Signature payment was discontinued. Make sure you know your PIN before traveling.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Cherry Blossom Packed Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Late March to mid-April: trains to popular hanami spots (Ueno, Meguro, Arashiyama) are extremely crowded on weekends. Go early morning.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Shinkansen Reserved vs Free
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Reserved seats (指定席) guarantee a spot. Unreserved (自由席) are cheaper but may require standing during peak times. Reserved recommended for weekends.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Ask Before Photographing People
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Always ask permission before photographing individuals, especially in traditional dress like kimono or yukata. A bow and camera gesture works.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Off-Peak Benefits
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Some passes offer off-peak discounts. Starting your day at 10 AM instead of 8 AM means cheaper fares and empty trains on some lines.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Always Choose Yen at ATMs — Avoid Dynamic Currency Conversion
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: When ATMs ask 'Home Currency' or 'Local Currency,' always choose yen. Choosing your home currency triggers a 3-7% markup through dynamic currency conversion.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Avoid Rush Hour
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Weekday trains from 7:30-9:30 and 17:30-19:30 are extremely crowded. Travel before 7:00 or after 10:00 for a comfortable ride.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Women-Only Train Cars
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many trains have women-only cars during rush hours (marked in pink). Men should not enter these cars during designated times.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Free Shinkansen Reservations
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: With JR Pass, you can reserve shinkansen seats for free at JR ticket counters. Reserved cars are less crowded than non-reserved, especially during peak times.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Less Crowded Lines Exist
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Parallel routes are often less crowded. In Tokyo, Tokyo Metro is usually less packed than JR. Check alternatives on Google Maps.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: eSIM Is Cheaper and Simpler Than Pocket WiFi for Solo/Couples
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: eSIM packages start at ~$5 for 1GB/7 days with instant activation. No device to carry or return. Most 2018+ phones support eSIM. Pocket WiFi only wins for groups of 3+.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Speak Softly in Public
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Maintain a quiet voice in public spaces, especially on trains and in museums. Loud conversations are considered impolite.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Basic Japanese Helps
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Learn: Arigatou (thanks), Sumimasen (excuse me/sorry), Kudasai (please give me). Even basic attempts at Japanese are appreciated.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Locker Strategy
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Train stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー). Sizes range from ¥300-700. Large sizes fill up fast - arrive early. Some accept IC cards. Note locker number!

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Chotto Means No
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: 'Chotto...' (ちょっと - 'a little...') with trailing off actually means 'no' or 'that's difficult.' Japanese rarely say 'no' directly. Learn to read the hesitation.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: US Travelers Need No Power Adapter — Everyone Else Does
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan uses Type A/B plugs (flat two-prong), identical to US/Canada. European, UK, and Australian plugs won't fit. Modern phone chargers (100-240V) work fine. Hair dryers may not.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Obon Rush (Mid-August)
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Obon (Aug 13-16) is a major travel period. Trains and Shinkansen fill up as people return to hometowns. Book early or enjoy emptier cities.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Express vs Local Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Check train type before boarding. Express (急行) and rapid (快速) skip stations. Local (各停/各駅停車) stops everywhere. Wrong train = missed stop.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Christmas in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Christmas isn't a holiday but is celebrated commercially. KFC for dinner is tradition! Couples treat it like Valentine's Day. Stores close normal hours.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: New Year Transit Schedules
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Dec 31 to Jan 3: many trains run all night on New Year's Eve, then reduced schedules Jan 1-3. Check your line's holiday timetable.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Bicycle Parking Tickets
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Bicycles parked illegally get ticketed or impounded (¥2000-3000 to retrieve). Use designated parking areas. Rental bikes often include parking spot info.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Japanese OTC Medicines Are Milder Than Western Equivalents
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Pain relievers, cold medicine, and allergy pills may feel weaker than what you're used to. Matsumoto Kiyoshi pharmacists can help select options even with limited English.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Summer Heat Precautions
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese summers are hot and humid (35°C+). Carry a hand towel, stay hydrated, and use cooling products from convenience stores. Heat stroke is real.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Bowing on Phone is Normal
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese people bow while talking on the phone - even though the other person can't see. It's unconscious and shows how ingrained bowing is. You'll catch yourself doing it!

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Pseudoephedrine (Sudafed, Vicks Inhaler) Is BANNED in Japan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Pseudoephedrine is classified as a stimulant raw material. Possession can lead to arrest. Leave Sudafed, Actifed, and Vicks nasal inhalers at home — buy Japanese cold medicine instead.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Clinics Closed Wednesdays
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many clinics and small hospitals close on Wednesday afternoons and Sundays. For emergencies, large hospitals have 24/7 services. Save emergency numbers in your phone.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Tatemae vs Honne
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese culture distinguishes between public face (tatemae) and true feelings (honne). People may say yes but mean no. Pay attention to context and body language.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Slipper Rules at Ryokan
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Use different slippers for different areas: regular slippers indoors, toilet slippers in bathrooms, and no slippers on tatami mats.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Prescription Medications Need Advance Documentation
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: ADHD drugs, strong painkillers, and certain anxiety medications require an import certificate from Japan's Ministry of Health — apply at least 2 weeks before travel.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Mobile Suica Option
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: iPhone users can add a mobile Suica via Apple Wallet — no physical card needed. Android users can use Google Pay with mobile Suica.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Download Safety Tips App for Earthquake Warnings
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Free JNTO app providing English earthquake early warnings, tsunami alerts, and heat stroke advisories with push notifications. Also download NHK World for live emergency broadcasts.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Station Signs Are Color-Coded
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Each rail line has a unique color and letter code (e.g., G for Ginza Line). Station numbers go up in one direction. Follow colors to navigate.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Check Your Hotel's Evacuation Route on Check-In
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese hotels post evacuation floor plans on room doors. Note the nearest stairway (not elevator) and the outdoor assembly point. Earthquakes happen without warning.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Summer Heat Stroke Is a Genuine Medical Risk
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: July-August regularly exceeds 35°C with extreme humidity. Carry water, rest in air-conditioned konbini, and buy OS-1 electrolyte drinks at any pharmacy for real rehydration.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Travel Insurance Should Cover Japan Specifically
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Doctor visits cost ¥5,000-10,000. ER visits can cost hundreds of thousands of yen. Ensure your policy covers Japan, includes medical evacuation, and covers natural disaster trip disruption.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: The Weak Yen Makes 2025-2026 Japan Travel Historically Affordable
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Budget travelers can manage ~$100/day including accommodation, food, and transport. Quality sushi, ryokan stays, and experiences cost significantly less than any time since 2013.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Green Car is First Class
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Green cars (グリーン車) on trains are first class - you need a special ticket. Don't accidentally sit there with a regular ticket or you'll pay the difference.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Google Maps for Transit
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Google Maps shows real-time Japan train schedules, platform numbers, and transfer walking times. It's the best navigation tool for transit.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Priority Seats (Silver Seats)
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Priority seats (優先席) near train doors are for elderly, pregnant, disabled, and injured passengers. Give them up if someone needs it.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Blowing Nose in Public
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Blowing your nose loudly in public is considered rude. Step away or use the restroom. Sniffling is actually preferred to blowing.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: No Eating on Local Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Eating and drinking on local commuter trains is considered rude. Shinkansen and limited express trains are fine — ekiben (station bento) are a tradition.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Japanese Addresses — No Street Names
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most Japanese addresses use a block system (chome), not street names. Building #30 might sit between #12 and #45. Always navigate by Google Maps pin, not address text.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Train Melody Departure Warning
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: The jingle playing at stations means the train is about to leave. Don't run for it - another train comes soon. Running causes accidents and delays for everyone.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Shinkansen Seat Etiquette
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Recline your seat with consideration - check behind you first. Return seat to upright before getting off. Keep belongings off empty seats.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: IC Card Basics
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Suica, PASMO, and Icoca are interchangeable across Japan. Tap in and out at ticket gates. Works on trains, buses, and at convenience stores.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Load IC Card at Machines
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Add money to your IC card at any station ticket machine. Look for the English button. Minimum charge is ¥1,000. Max balance ¥20,000.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: JR Pass Worth It?
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: A 7-day JR Pass (¥50,000) pays off with 3+ long Shinkansen trips. For Tokyo-Kyoto-Osaka only, individual tickets may be cheaper.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Last Train Around Midnight
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Most trains stop around midnight (23:30–0:30). Check your last train time — missing it means a taxi or manga cafe until 5 AM first trains.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Transfer Arrows on Platforms
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Colored floor arrows and signs point to connecting lines. Follow the line color you need. Major stations have English transfer signs.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Hyperdia for Timetables
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Hyperdia and Jorudan apps show detailed train timetables, fares, and platform info. Filter by JR-only if you have a JR Pass.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Phone on Manner Mode
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Set your phone to manner mode (silent) on trains. Phone calls are frowned upon — text instead. Near priority seats, turn your phone off.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Station Staff Speak English
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Major station staff at info counters usually speak basic English. Look for the green '?' information windows. Platform intercom buttons connect to staff.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Stroller-Friendly Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Shinkansen and newer trains have priority spaces for strollers near doors. Elevators exist at most stations but may require a detour.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Locker Finder Apps
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Ecbo Cloak and Coin Locker Navi apps show available lockers and baggage storage near you. Ecbo Cloak also lists shops that store bags.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Luggage Delivery Counter
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Station delivery counters (Sagawa, Yamato) can send bags to your next hotel or the airport. Ask your hotel or look for the delivery counter sign.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Drink Vending on Platforms
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Platform vending machines sell drinks for ¥130-170. Hot and cold options. Look for the red (hot) and blue (cold) indicators. A lifesaver while waiting.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Bathroom Before Boarding
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Use the restroom at the station — local trains don't have toilets. Shinkansen and limited express do. Station restrooms are free and clean.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Evening Rush Shorter
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Evening rush (17:30-19:30) is shorter and less intense than morning rush. Trains are crowded but move steadily. Consider delaying departure 30 min.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: First Train at 5 AM
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: First trains run around 5:00-5:30 AM. Early starts let you catch sunrise at temples and avoid crowds at popular spots like Fushimi Inari.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Suica Works Nationwide
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Despite different IC card brands by region (Suica, Icoca, PASMO, Kitaca, etc.), they all work interchangeably on any transit system nationwide.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Children's Fare Half Price
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Children aged 6-11 pay half fare on trains. Under 6 ride free (up to 2 per adult). JR child passes are also available at half price.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Underground Shopping Streets
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Major stations connect to underground shopping streets (地下街). Useful during rain. Tokyo Station, Shinjuku, Umeda, and Nagoya have extensive ones.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Takkyubin Luggage Forwarding
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Send luggage ahead via takkyubin (Yamato kuroneko or Sagawa) for ¥2,000-3,000 per box. Drop off at any konbini or hotel front desk — arrives next day at your next hotel. Travel hands-free.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: April Rain Showers
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: April averages 10-12 rainy days in Tokyo. Pack a compact umbrella and waterproof shoes. Convenience stores sell transparent umbrellas for ¥500-700 if you forget.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: March Weather: Layers Are Essential
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: March temps range 5-15°C in Tokyo and Osaka. Mornings are cold, afternoons mild. Pack a light down jacket and a waterproof layer for unpredictable showers.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Child Train Fares and IC Cards
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Kids under 6 ride free on trains (up to 2 per adult). Ages 6-11 pay half fare. Child IC cards (kodomo PASMO/Suica) auto-deduct half. Shinkansen: under-6 free if sharing a seat.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Taxi Services
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan Taxi (JapanTaxi app) lets you book Universal Design taxis with ramps. ¥410-730 base fare. Available in all major cities — request UD vehicle when booking.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Shinkansen Seats — Book Early
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Wheelchair spaces on shinkansen are in Car 11 (Tokaido/Sanyo) or Car 7 (Tohoku). Book 2+ days ahead at Midori no Madoguchi — only 1-2 spaces per train.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Rainy Season (Tsuyu) Arrives Mid-June
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Tsuyu hits Kyushu around June 5, Kansai/Kanto by June 10, and Tohoku by mid-June. Hokkaido skips tsuyu entirely. Expect 15-20 rainy days this month.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Locker Strategy for Luggage-Free Days
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Coin lockers at every station cost ¥300 (small), ¥500 (medium), ¥700-800 (large) for 24hrs. IC card or coins. Ecbo Cloak app reserves space at nearby shops when lockers are full.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Nap Strategy on Trains and Buses
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Schedule long train rides during nap time. Shinkansen hum and smooth ride lull kids to sleep. Bring a lightweight blanket — trains run cold AC in summer. Reserved seats guarantee the window.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Locker Your Camera Bag at Stations
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Station coin lockers (¥400-700) free you from heavy camera bags while exploring. Small ¥400 lockers fit a day bag; large ¥700 lockers hold full camera backpacks.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Lockers Near Entertainment Districts
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Stash bags, shopping, and jackets in station coin lockers before a night out. ¥400-700 per locker, available at every station. Some accept IC cards with codes — no key to lose.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Last Train Times and Planning Around Them
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Last trains run roughly midnight-12:30am in most cities. Miss it and you're looking at ¥5,000-15,000 taxi fares or a capsule hotel. Check your exact last train on the Navitime app.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Train Etiquette
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Phone on silent ("manner mode"), no phone calls on trains, don't eat on local trains (shinkansen is OK), yield priority seats (yūsen zaseki). Queuing lines are painted on the platform.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Trains Are Green Transport
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan's rail network is almost entirely electrified and one of the most energy-efficient transport systems in the world. Choosing trains over domestic flights cuts your trip's carbon footprint dram...

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Hospital Visits for Tourists
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Bring your passport and travel insurance card. Say "eigo dekiru isha" for an English-speaking doctor. Pay upfront (¥5,000-15,000 consultation), claim insurance reimbursement later.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Golden Hour Timing by Season
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan golden hour shifts dramatically: summer sunrise ~4:30am/sunset ~7pm, winter sunrise ~6:50am/sunset ~4:30pm. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable shooting windows.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Typhoon Season Awareness
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Typhoons hit Japan Aug-Oct, mainly affecting western/southern regions. Monitor forecasts, have flexible plans, and know your hotel's emergency procedures.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: June Hotel Discounts: Low Season Pricing
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: June is Japan's cheapest month for accommodation outside Hokkaido. Expect 20-40% off peak rates in Kyoto, Tokyo, and Osaka. Ryokan offer midweek deals.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Allergy Season is Brutal
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Cedar pollen (sugi) season in Feb-April is intense. Locals wear masks, take medicine, and suffer. If you have allergies, bring medication - local antihistamines are mild.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Warmer Temps but Still Cool Evenings
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Daytime highs reach 18-22°C in Tokyo/Osaka by late April, but evenings drop to 10-12°C. Bring a light jacket for yozakura and evening walks.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: October Is Peak Season: Book Now
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: October is Japan's highest-demand month for tourism. Hotels in Kyoto, Tokyo, and Hakone book out 2-3 months ahead. Prices rise 40-60% over September.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Indoor AC is Freezing
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japanese buildings are heavily air-conditioned in summer - bring a light jacket for trains, malls, and restaurants. The contrast with outdoor heat is shocking.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Bike Rental Over Taxi for Short Distances
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many cities offer rental bicycles from ¥200-500/day or bike-share systems from ¥150/30min. Kyoto, Nara, Kanazawa, and Matsuyama are especially bike-friendly. Zero emissions, more fun.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Coin Lockers — Sizes, Payment, and Finding Them
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Station coin lockers: small ¥300 (daypack), medium ¥500 (cabin bag), large ¥700 (suitcase). IC card or coin operated. Use the Coin Locker Navi app — popular stations fill by noon.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Carbon Offset for Domestic Flights
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: If flying domestically (Tokyo-Okinawa, Tokyo-Sapporo), ANA and JAL offer carbon offset programs at checkout. ¥500-1,000 per flight supports renewable energy and forest projects.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Overnight Buses Save Money
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Highway buses (Willer, JR Bus) between cities cost ¥3,000-6,000 — half the Shinkansen price. Overnight buses save a hotel night too.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Smart EX for Shinkansen
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Smart EX app lets you book Shinkansen tickets online and board with your IC card — no paper tickets needed. Register with a non-Japanese card.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Accessible Travel Support
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Major stations have elevators and accessible gates. Station staff will deploy ramps for wheelchair boarding. Call ahead for smaller stations.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: SIM Card and eSIM Setup
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Get an eSIM before arrival (Ubigi, Airalo, IIJmio) or buy a physical SIM at airport vending machines or Bic Camera. Data-only SIMs from ¥1,500/7 days. Activate before you clear customs.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: WiFi on Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Shinkansen offer free WiFi (JR-EAST_FREE_WiFi). Coverage can be spotty in tunnels. Local trains generally don't have WiFi. Download offline maps.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Fare Adjustment Machines
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: If you exit with insufficient IC card balance or wrong ticket, use the fare adjustment machine (精算機) near the gates before passing through.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Trains Run On Time
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Japan's trains are famously punctual. If your train is scheduled for 10:03, it leaves at 10:03. Be on the platform 2-3 minutes early.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Delay Certificates
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: If your train is delayed 5+ minutes, staff hand out delay certificates (遅延証明書) at the exit. These excuse lateness for work or appointments.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Platform Benches Face Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Platform benches face the track deliberately. The armrests prevent sleeping. During rush hour, benches may be folded up to make standing room.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Weekend Trains Are Relaxed
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Weekend trains are significantly less crowded than weekdays. Sightseeing routes (Kamakura, Nikko, Hakone) are the exception — those get busy.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Night Bus Rest Stops
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Overnight highway buses stop every 2-3 hours at service areas (SA) with restrooms, vending machines, and sometimes convenience stores.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Lost Item on Train
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Left something on a train? Report it to station staff immediately. JR's lost-and-found rate is remarkable — most items are returned.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Capsule Hotel Quiet Hours
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Capsule hotels enforce strict quiet hours (usually 10 PM - 7 AM). No phone calls, alarms should be vibration-only, and conversations in whispers.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Earthquake Train Stops
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Trains automatically stop during earthquakes. Stay calm and follow crew instructions. Service usually resumes within 30-60 minutes after checking tracks.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Platform Screen Doors
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Many Metro stations have platform screen doors (ホームドア) that open with the train doors. Stand behind the yellow line until doors open.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Ryokan Arrival Timing
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Arrive at ryokan between 3-6 PM. Dinner is often served at a set time. Late arrivals should notify the ryokan in advance.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Seat Heaters in Winter
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Train seats in winter often have seat heaters. The warmth is subtle — it's not broken, that's by design. Enjoy the cozy ride.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Golden Week Trains
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Golden Week (Apr 29–May 5) is peak domestic travel. Shinkansen sells out. Book reserved seats 1 month ahead or travel on off-peak days.

universalNoCategoryTips.title: Luggage Forwarding with Takkyubin
universalNoCategoryTips.summary: Yamato Transport (Kuroneko) ships suitcases between hotels for ¥1,500-2,500 per bag. Drop off at any konbini or hotel front desk. Arrives next day. Travel light on train days with kids.

locationInsiderData.name: Lucky Pierrot Goryokaku Koen Mae
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Best visited in the evening when the atmosphere comes alive. Making a reservation for dinner is recommended.

locationInsiderData.name: Mabuni Hills
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The information boards tell a story the stones alone can't. Take time to read them — context transforms the experience.

locationInsiderData.name: Daikokuya Genghis Khan
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Best visited in the evening when the atmosphere comes alive. Making a reservation for dinner is recommended.

locationInsiderData.name: Iya Onsen Hotel Restaurant
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: This is a local favorite — expect a queue during peak hours. Weekday lunches are usually the sweet spot.

locationInsiderData.name: Maruo Onsen
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The rotenburo (outdoor bath) is the highlight — time it for early morning or sunset.

locationInsiderData.name: Site of Kanayama Castle
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The keep stairs are steep and narrow — take your time and hold the railing. Socks are required inside (shoes off at the entrance).

locationInsiderData.name: Fugaku Fuketsu Wind Cave
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Allow at least half a day here. Bring water, sunscreen, and proper footwear — the terrain can be uneven.

locationInsiderData.name: Kankakei Ropeway
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Arrive early or visit at night to dodge the heaviest crowds. The landmark is photogenic at all hours but the light changes everything.

locationInsiderData.name: Maguro monument (Tuna Statue of Honshu North Edge)
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Arrive early or visit at night to dodge the heaviest crowds. The landmark is photogenic at all hours but the light changes everything.

locationInsiderData.name: Tsukimachi Waterfall
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Allow at least half a day here. Bring water, sunscreen, and proper footwear — the terrain can be uneven.

locationInsiderData.name: Koubai
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: This is a local favorite — expect a queue during peak hours. Weekday lunches are usually the sweet spot.

locationInsiderData.name: Mashiko Museum of Ceramic Art
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Visit the museum first floor before your workshop — seeing Hamada's work inspires your own pottery.

locationInsiderData.name: Panorama Park Nishiharadai
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The area around the landmark is often just as interesting. Budget time to explore the surrounding streets and shops.

locationInsiderData.name: Port Of Nagoya Public Aquarium
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Feeding times are the highlight — check the schedule at the entrance and plan your route around them.

locationInsiderData.name: Chano-ma Hiroshima
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: A neighborhood institution — regulars take the counter seats. Don't rush; the pace here is deliberately slow.

locationInsiderData.name: Manekineko Museum
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The interactive exhibits here are worth seeking out — they bring the subject alive in a way display cases can't.

locationInsiderData.name: Magome (Nakasendo)
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: The information boards tell a story the stones alone can't. Take time to read them — context transforms the experience.

locationInsiderData.name: Nōgakuden (Noh Stage)
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Purify your hands at the temizuya before approaching the main hall — left hand first, then right, then pour water into your left hand to rinse your mouth.

locationInsiderData.name: Fujisan Gate
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Allow at least half a day here. Bring water, sunscreen, and proper footwear — the terrain can be uneven.

locationInsiderData.name: Mato Park
locationInsiderData.insider_tip: Time your visit to catch the seasonal display — cherry blossoms, azaleas, or autumn leaves transform the park completely.

cashRelatedTips.title: Use Both Hands for Exchanges
