cashRelatedTips.summary: Present business cards, gifts, and money with both hands. Receive items the same way. Shows respect and gratitude.

cashRelatedTips.title: Miyajima Floating Torii
cashRelatedTips.summary: The torii gate is sacred - don't climb on it or throw coins at it. Best photos at high tide (gate in water) or low tide (walk to gate).

cashRelatedTips.title: Baseball Game Atmosphere
cashRelatedTips.summary: Japanese baseball fans are organized - each team has coordinated cheers and songs. Join in! Trumpets and drums are common. It's louder than you'd expect.

cashRelatedTips.title: Pachinko Parlor Basics
cashRelatedTips.summary: Pachinko is gambling-adjacent and can be overwhelming. It's extremely loud. Winnings are exchanged for prizes, then traded for cash at nearby shops.

cashRelatedTips.title: Standing Bars Are Cheap
cashRelatedTips.summary: Tachinomi (立ち飲み standing bars) are much cheaper than seated izakayas. Popular with salarymen after work. No table charge, quick service, local atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Night Buses Save Money
cashRelatedTips.summary: Overnight highway buses between cities cost 1/3 of shinkansen fares. Not glamorous but practical. Book through Willer Express or Japan Bus Lines.

cashRelatedTips.title: Shrine Offering Etiquette
cashRelatedTips.summary: Gently toss a coin into the saisenbako at shrines. A 5 yen coin (go-en) is often chosen for good luck.

cashRelatedTips.title: Maintain Shrine Serenity
cashRelatedTips.summary: Keep your voice down and maintain a quiet demeanor within shrine grounds. Loud noise can disturb the sacred atmosphere and other visitors.

cashRelatedTips.title: Maintain Quiet Reflection
cashRelatedTips.summary: Speak softly and keep phones silent on temple grounds to preserve the peaceful atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Capture Temple Gardens Respectfully
cashRelatedTips.summary: Photograph the tranquil temple gardens, maintaining their serene atmosphere for others.

cashRelatedTips.title: Expect Boisterous Dining
cashRelatedTips.summary: Osaka is known for its lively and sometimes loud dining culture, especially in izakayas and local eateries. Embrace the energetic atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Quiet Contemplation in Zen Gardens
cashRelatedTips.summary: Maintain silence and a respectful demeanor in Kyoto's Zen gardens, such as Ryoan-ji or Daitoku-ji, to preserve their serene atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Explore Nishiki Market for Free
cashRelatedTips.summary: Wander through Nishiki Market, Kyoto's "Kitchen," for free, soaking in the lively local atmosphere and discovering unique ingredients.

cashRelatedTips.title: Master the Kyoto City Bus
cashRelatedTips.summary: Utilize Kyoto's extensive bus network; purchase a One-Day Bus Pass (approx. 700 yen) for unlimited rides within the city zone, saving money.

cashRelatedTips.title: Use the Osaka Metro Day Pass
cashRelatedTips.summary: For extensive inner-city travel, consider a 1-day Osaka Metro Pass for unlimited rides on subway lines, saving money compared to single tickets.

cashRelatedTips.title: Carry Cash for Smaller Bars
cashRelatedTips.summary: Many independent and older Japanese bars operate on a cash-only basis, so always have enough yen on hand.

cashRelatedTips.title: Understand Mama-san / Master Role
cashRelatedTips.summary: In smaller, owner-operated bars, the proprietor (Mama-san for women, Master for men) plays a significant role in creating the atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Bring Small Denominations and Coins
cashRelatedTips.summary: Many market stalls, especially smaller ones, prefer cash and may not have change for large bills like ¥10,000, so carry smaller notes and coins.

cashRelatedTips.title: Use Coin Lockers for Large Bags
cashRelatedTips.summary: Utilize coin lockers (koin rokka) often found near museum entrances to store large bags, backpacks, or coats.

cashRelatedTips.title: Practice Silent Reflection
cashRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet, contemplative atmosphere in Zen gardens and meditation spaces to respect others seeking tranquility.

cashRelatedTips.title: Maximize Hakodate Tram with Day Pass
cashRelatedTips.summary: Save money and travel efficiently on Hakodate's tram system by purchasing a 1-day or 2-day pass for unlimited rides.

cashRelatedTips.title: Maximize with Fukuoka Subway Day Pass
cashRelatedTips.summary: Purchase a Fukuoka City Subway One-Day Pass for 640 yen (weekdays) or 520 yen (weekends/holidays) for unlimited rides, a cost-effective way to explore.

cashRelatedTips.title: Maintain Quiet at D.T. Suzuki Museum
cashRelatedTips.summary: Preserve the serene atmosphere of the D.T. Suzuki Museum, especially in the Reflection Space, by maintaining respectful silence.

cashRelatedTips.title: Capture Higashi Chaya's Morning Glow
cashRelatedTips.summary: Photograph Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya District early in the morning for soft light, fewer crowds, and a more authentic atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Seek Daily Lunch Specials
cashRelatedTips.summary: Save money by opting for teishoku (set meals) or daily lunch specials (higawari) offered by many Hiroshima restaurants during weekdays.

cashRelatedTips.title: Nagasaki Streetcar Day Pass
cashRelatedTips.summary: Purchase a 1-day streetcar pass for 600 yen for unlimited rides. This saves money if you plan more than 4 rides in a day.

cashRelatedTips.title: Nagasaki Tram Etiquette
cashRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet on Nagasaki's streetcars and pay the flat fare (140 yen) when exiting. Respect others' space in the typically crowded trams.

cashRelatedTips.title: Kyoto Station Coin Lockers
cashRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto Station has large lockers on B1 near the Shinkansen gate and near the Tourist Info Center. They fill up fast — arrive before 10 AM.

cashRelatedTips.title: Photograph Ishite-ji Archways
cashRelatedTips.summary: Capture the atmospheric tunnel of ancient archways (manga-do) leading to the main hall at Ishite-ji Temple, a unique photo opportunity.

cashRelatedTips.title: Ride Matsuyama City Tram Pass
cashRelatedTips.summary: Maximize your sightseeing with the Matsuyama City Tram 1-Day Pass (Adult: 600 yen), offering unlimited rides on the city's network.

cashRelatedTips.title: Capture Kokusai Dori's Vibrancy
cashRelatedTips.summary: Photograph the lively atmosphere of Kokusai Dori, Naha's main street, especially in the evening when neon lights illuminate the unique shops and street performers.

cashRelatedTips.title: Capture Susukino at Night
cashRelatedTips.summary: Head to Susukino, Sapporo's vibrant entertainment district, after dark to capture its neon-lit streets and bustling nightlife atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Respect Sankeien Serenity
cashRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet and avoid littering to preserve the tranquil atmosphere of Sankeien Garden, a historic Japanese landscape garden in Yokohama.

cashRelatedTips.title: Enjoy Jozenji-dori Illuminations
cashRelatedTips.summary: During winter, Jozenji-dori Avenue transforms with beautiful light illuminations, creating a magical atmosphere. This event typically runs from mid-December to early January.

cashRelatedTips.title: Visit Sendai Morning Market
cashRelatedTips.summary: Experience the lively atmosphere and fresh local produce at Sendai Morning Market (Sendai Asaichi), a great place to find seasonal fruits, vegetables, and seafood.

cashRelatedTips.title: Explore Takamatsu by Bicycle
cashRelatedTips.summary: Rent a bicycle from spots like Takamatsu Port (around 500-1000 yen/day) or near major stations for flexible exploration of Ritsurin Garden and city sights.

cashRelatedTips.title: Ritsurin Garden Accessibility
cashRelatedTips.summary: Ritsurin Garden offers relatively accessible main paths, though some sections are gravel. Wheelchair rentals are available at the East Gate for easier exploration (approx. 300 yen).

cashRelatedTips.title: Rural Buses: Board Rear, Pay Front, Carry Coins
cashRelatedTips.summary: Outside cities, board from the rear door and take a numbered ticket. Pay the exact fare at the front when exiting. Many rural buses accept only cash — carry coins.

cashRelatedTips.title: Cash Tray Protocol at Registers
cashRelatedTips.summary: Place cash on the small tray (cashier tray) at the register, never hand money directly to the cashier.

cashRelatedTips.title: Konbini Essentials You Didn't Know You Needed
cashRelatedTips.summary: 7-Eleven, Lawson, and FamilyMart stock onigiri, bento, ATMs, ticket printers, and even decent coffee for under ¥200.

cashRelatedTips.title: IC Card for Vending Machines and Small Purchases
cashRelatedTips.summary: Charge your Suica or PASMO at any station kiosk and tap to pay at vending machines, konbini, and coin lockers.

cashRelatedTips.title: 100-Yen Shops for Travel Supplies
cashRelatedTips.summary: Daiso, Seria, and Can Do sell travel-sized toiletries, chopsticks, stationery, and souvenirs starting at ¥110.

cashRelatedTips.title: Reserve Coin Lockers with the SPACER App
cashRelatedTips.summary: Large lockers vanish by late morning at major stations during peak seasons. The SPACER app shows real-time availability and lets you reserve a locker before arriving.

cashRelatedTips.title: Coin Locker Fees Reset at Midnight
cashRelatedTips.summary: Locker charges are per calendar day, not per 24 hours. Storing bags at 11 PM and retrieving at 1 AM costs two days of rental. Maximum storage is 3 days.

cashRelatedTips.title: Use the Ticket Machine Before You Sit
cashRelatedTips.summary: Most ramen shops use a ticket machine (shokkenki) at the entrance. Insert cash, press the button for your bowl, and hand the ticket to the cook.

cashRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Coin Lockers
cashRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Station has large coin lockers near the Shinkansen gates (B1 floor). For hard-to-find lockers, check the Coin Locker Navi app.

cashRelatedTips.title: Experience Sumida River Fireworks
cashRelatedTips.summary: Plan your summer visit to Tokyo to coincide with the spectacular Sumida River Fireworks Festival, usually held in late July.

cashRelatedTips.title: Visit Omicho Market Early
cashRelatedTips.summary: Explore Kanazawa's Omicho Market in the early morning for the freshest seafood and a lively, less crowded atmosphere.

cashRelatedTips.title: Never Tip — It May Cause Confusion or Offense
cashRelatedTips.summary: Tipping is not customary in Japan. If you leave money on the table, staff may chase you down thinking you forgot it. This applies to all services: restaurants, taxis, hotels.

cashRelatedTips.title: Coin Laundry Saves Packing
cashRelatedTips.summary: Coin laundry (koin randorī) costs ¥200-400 per wash and ¥100 per 10 minutes of drying. Found everywhere — Google Maps 'コインランドリー' to find the nearest one. Pack light and wash often.

cashRelatedTips.title: Coin Lockers at Stations
cashRelatedTips.summary: Most stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー) in 3 sizes: small (¥400), medium (¥500), large (¥700). Pay with IC card or coins. 3-day max.

cashRelatedTips.title: Coin-Operated Rides, Arcades, and Game Centers
cashRelatedTips.summary: Kiddie rides outside supermarkets cost ¥100-200. Game centers (Sega, Namco) have claw machines and photo booths from ¥100. Arcades in Akihabara and Den Den Town are kid magnets.

cashRelatedTips.title: Cash-Only vs Cashless Bars
cashRelatedTips.summary: Small bars (5-10 seats) are almost always cash-only. Larger bars and chains accept IC cards and credit cards. Carry at least ¥10,000 in cash for a night out. ATMs at konbini work 24/7.

cashRelatedTips.title: Bar Etiquette: Volume and Behavior
cashRelatedTips.summary: Japanese bars value a calm atmosphere. Keep your voice conversational. Don't shout across the room or between tables. Toasting (kanpai!) is fine — sustained rowdiness is not.

cashRelatedTips.title: Osaka: Dotonbori Never Sleeps
cashRelatedTips.summary: Dotonbori's neon strip and surrounding bars stay alive well past midnight. Hozenji Yokocho alley has atmospheric tiny bars. Amerikamura (Ame-mura) has the club and DJ scene.

cashRelatedTips.title: Hakodate: Bay Area Evening and Wine Bars
cashRelatedTips.summary: Hakodate's Bay Area has converted warehouse wine bars and cafes with canal views. Evenings are quiet and atmospheric — pair with the Mt. Hakodate night view for a complete evening.

cashRelatedTips.title: Gion Matsuri Lasts All of July, Not Just the Parade Days
cashRelatedTips.summary: While the famous float parade happens July 17 and 24, the wider festival spans all of July. The yoiyama evening street markets (July 14-16, 21-23) are often more atmospheric.

cashRelatedTips.title: Japan Is 43% Cashless — But You Still Need Cash
cashRelatedTips.summary: Credit cards work at chains and department stores, but small ramen shops, izakayas, street food stalls, rural ryokans, and shrine admission often require cash. Carry ¥10,000-20,000.

cashRelatedTips.title: Cash is Still King
cashRelatedTips.summary: Many small shops, restaurants, and traditional businesses are cash-only. Always carry ¥10,000-20,000 in cash. ATMs at convenience stores accept foreign cards.

cashRelatedTips.title: Credit Card PINs Now Required — No More Signatures
cashRelatedTips.summary: Since April 2025, IC chip credit card transactions require your 4-digit PIN. Signature payment was discontinued. Make sure you know your PIN before traveling.

cashRelatedTips.title: 7-Eleven ATMs Best for Foreigners
cashRelatedTips.summary: 7-Eleven and Japan Post ATMs are most reliable for foreign cards. Regular bank ATMs often reject overseas cards. Look for 'International ATM' signs.

cashRelatedTips.title: Always Choose Yen at ATMs — Avoid Dynamic Currency Conversion
cashRelatedTips.summary: When ATMs ask 'Home Currency' or 'Local Currency,' always choose yen. Choosing your home currency triggers a 3-7% markup through dynamic currency conversion.

cashRelatedTips.title: Koyasan Okunoin Cemetery
cashRelatedTips.summary: Walk respectfully through Japan's largest cemetery. Don't step on graves, speak quietly, and don't disturb offerings. Most atmospheric at dusk or dawn.

cashRelatedTips.title: Coin Locker Strategy
cashRelatedTips.summary: Train stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー). Sizes range from ¥300-700. Large sizes fill up fast - arrive early. Some accept IC cards. Note locker number!

cashRelatedTips.title: 100 Yen Shops Quality
cashRelatedTips.summary: Daiso, Seria, and Can Do (100 yen shops) have surprisingly good quality items. Great for travel essentials, souvenirs, snacks, and random necessities.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Priority Seating Respect
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Priority seats near train doors are for elderly, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. Offer your seat when needed.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Bowing Basics
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: A slight bow (15°) is appropriate for casual greetings. Deeper bows show more respect. As a tourist, a small nod or bow is appreciated.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Temple Prayer Differs from Shrines
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: At Buddhist temples: bow, put hands together (no clapping), pray silently, then bow again. Clapping is only for Shinto shrines.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Oshibori Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Use the oshibori (wet towel) only to clean your hands, not your face or neck. Fold it neatly after use and place it back on the tray.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Lift Bowl When Eating
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: It's proper to lift rice and soup bowls close to your mouth when eating. This is different from Western table manners.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Finish Your Rice
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Try to finish all rice in your bowl. Leaving rice is considered wasteful. It's OK to ask for smaller portions if you can't finish.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Convenience Store Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Eat purchased food at the store's designated area or outside - not while walking. Separate trash properly using provided bins.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Use Both Hands for Exchanges
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Present business cards, gifts, and money with both hands. Receive items the same way. Shows respect and gratitude.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Umbrella Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Shake wet umbrellas outside and use provided umbrella bags or lockers. Don't bring dripping umbrellas into shops or trains.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Queue Jumping is Taboo
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Never cut in line. Japanese queuing culture is serious - people queue quietly and patiently, even for hours. Respect the order.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Tsukiji Outer Market Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Don't block vendor stalls while eating. Eat at designated areas or standing by the stall. Don't touch products without purchasing.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Nara Deer Interaction
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Bow to deer and they'll bow back! Buy official shika senbei (deer crackers) only. Don't feed human food or tease the deer - they can bite!

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Geisha District Manners
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: In geisha districts (Gion, Pontocho), don't chase, touch, or block geiko/maiko. Private streets exist - respect 'no entry' signs.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Feed Nara Deer Safely
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Purchase deer crackers (shika senbei) from vendors, bow to deer before feeding, and don't tease them. They can be assertive.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Overtourism Awareness
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Popular sites like Kyoto's Gion and Fushimi Inari face overtourism. Visit early/late, explore lesser-known alternatives, and respect local residents.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Kanazawa Geisha Districts
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya is less crowded than Kyoto's Gion but same etiquette applies. Evening visits offer chance to hear shamisen music from teahouses.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Dotonbori Photo Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: The Glico Man sign is for everyone - take your photo quickly and move on. Don't block the bridge or walkway for extended shoots.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Shirakawa-go Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: These are real homes - don't enter private property or peer into windows. Stay on designated paths. Winter illumination requires lottery reservation.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Kimono Rental Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Walk with smaller steps in kimono. Sit carefully to avoid wrinkling. Use restroom before dressing - it's complicated! Return on time to avoid fees.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Tea Ceremony Participation
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Accept the tea bowl with your right hand, place in left palm, rotate clockwise 2-3 times to avoid drinking from the front. Finish in 3 sips.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: TeamLab Booking Essential
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: TeamLab exhibits require advance tickets - often sold out weeks ahead. Wear comfortable shoes (lots of walking) and clothes you don't mind getting wet.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Don't Blow on Hot Food
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Blowing on hot food to cool it is considered poor manners. Wave your hand over it or wait patiently. For noodles, slurping helps cool them as you eat.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Watch the Opening Bow
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Department stores open at exactly 10 AM with staff lined up bowing to entering customers. It's a small cultural moment worth witnessing. Mitsukoshi is famous for this.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Public Green Space Conduct
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Enjoy Tokyo's parks responsibly. Don't leave trash, avoid loud activities, and respect designated areas, especially during peak seasons like cherry blossom.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Oshibori Isn't for Face
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: While some Japanese men wipe their face with the hot towel, it's technically improper etiquette. Hands only is the polite way, especially in nicer restaurants.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Sacred Site Photography
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When photographing at Tokyo's temples and shrines like Senso-ji, avoid disrupting worshippers and adhere to "no photography" signs in sacred areas.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Shrine Offering Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Gently toss a coin into the saisenbako at shrines. A 5 yen coin (go-en) is often chosen for good luck.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respectful Shrine Attire
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Dress modestly at shrines. Avoid overly revealing clothes, especially when entering sacred buildings, to show respect for the holy site.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Walk Along Shrine Sides
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Avoid walking directly down the center of the main path (sando) at shrines. Walk along the sides to show respect for the kami (deity).

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Prepare for Gravel Paths
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Expect extensive gravel paths at many shrines. Wear comfortable, sturdy shoes suitable for uneven surfaces and long walks to enjoy your visit.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Dress Modestly at Temples
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Cover shoulders and knees to show respect at sacred temple sites.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Remove Shoes in Temple Halls
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Always take off your shoes before entering designated temple halls, a fundamental sign of respect.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Don't Touch Temple Artifacts
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Observe statues and relics from a respectful distance without touching them to prevent damage.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Capture Temple Gardens Respectfully
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Photograph the tranquil temple gardens, maintaining their serene atmosphere for others.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Navigating Kitano Ijinkan-gai
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Wear comfortable shoes when exploring the Kitano Ijinkan-gai district due to its steep, cobbled streets and numerous uphill climbs to foreign residences.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Dining Etiquette in Nankinmachi
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: While enjoying street food in Nankinmachi (Chinatown), it's customary to eat directly at the stall or find designated eating areas, not walk while consuming.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Gion's Private Alleys
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Avoid entering private property or narrow alleyways in Gion, especially in areas like Hanamikoji. Respect "No Entry" signs displayed.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Quiet Contemplation in Zen Gardens
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain silence and a respectful demeanor in Kyoto's Zen gardens, such as Ryoan-ji or Daitoku-ji, to preserve their serene atmosphere.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Cover Mouth With Hand
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Cover your mouth discreetly if using a toothpick after your meal to show good manners.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Castle Visits with Kids
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Castle keep interiors have steep wooden stairs — hold hands with small children. Shoes come off at the entrance, so slip-on shoes save time. The grounds outside are often the highlight for kids.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Sacred Site Rules
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Follow designated paths, avoid loud conversations, and dress modestly when visiting Nara's many shrines and temples.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Standing Bar Etiquette (Tachinomi)
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When at an Osaka standing bar (tachinomi), be considerate of space, order efficiently, and move aside for new patrons if you're lingering.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Bartender's Craft
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Observe the bartender's skill without interruption. Don't demand quick service if they are busy crafting drinks.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Counter Seating
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When seated at a bar counter, be mindful of your personal space and avoid sprawling out or placing too many belongings on the limited counter space.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Don't Linger Without Intent to Buy
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: While browsing is welcome, avoid extended loitering or blocking stalls if you have no intention of making a purchase, respecting vendor space.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Exhibit Photography Rules
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Always check for "no photography" signs, especially near artifacts or in specific halls, as rules vary greatly between museums.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Speak Softly in Galleries
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a low voice and avoid loud conversations or phone calls while inside museum exhibition rooms to respect other visitors.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Practice Silent Reflection
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet, contemplative atmosphere in Zen gardens and meditation spaces to respect others seeking tranquility.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Observe Wildlife from Afar
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a respectful distance from wild animals, especially monkeys (saru) and deer (shika), to avoid disturbing them or provoking aggressive behavior.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Wear Appropriate Footwear
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: For nature excursions, wear sturdy, comfortable hiking shoes or boots with good grip, as many trails can be uneven, slippery, or steep.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Photography Restrictions at Sacred Nature Sites
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Some natural sites, particularly those with spiritual significance like sacred waterfalls or ancient forests, may have photography restrictions. Look for signs.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Savor Kaisendon Respectfully at Morning Market
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When enjoying a fresh seafood bowl (kaisendon) at Hakodate Morning Market, eat quickly and avoid lingering, especially during busy hours.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Sacred Photography Bans
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Observe strictly "No Photography" signs in sacred spaces like temple meditation halls, inner shrines, or certain onsen areas.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet at Peace Park
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a solemn and respectful demeanor within Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park and Museum. This is a site of deep historical significance.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Photograph Dazaifu Tenmangu Respectfully
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Capture the beauty of Dazaifu Tenmangu Shrine's grounds and main hall exterior, but refrain from photography during active rituals or inside sacred inner areas.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Observe Etiquette at Nanzoin Temple
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Show respect at Nanzoin Temple, home to a massive reclining Buddha. Maintain quiet, remove shoes where required, and follow specific photography rules.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Kanazawa Chaya District Privacy
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting Kanazawa's historic Chaya districts, refrain from disturbing residents or entering private properties, especially when geiko are present.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Maintain Quiet at D.T. Suzuki Museum
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Preserve the serene atmosphere of the D.T. Suzuki Museum, especially in the Reflection Space, by maintaining respectful silence.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Solo Onsen Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Onsen are ideal solo activities. No need for conversation — just bathe, soak, and relax. Tattoo-friendly onsen are listed on tattoo-friendly.jp if that's a concern.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Queues for Mt. Hakodate Ropeway
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When waiting for the Mt. Hakodate Ropeway, strictly adhere to designated queuing lines and avoid pushing, as it's a popular attraction.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: No Flash at Peace Museum
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Respect photography restrictions within the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, especially avoiding flash photography to preserve artifacts and show reverence.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respectful Peace Park Visit
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Observe solemnity at the Nagasaki Peace Park. Avoid loud conversations, boisterous behavior, and ensure respectful conduct in this memorial area.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Nagasaki Tram Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet on Nagasaki's streetcars and pay the flat fare (140 yen) when exiting. Respect others' space in the typically crowded trams.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Atsuta Jingu Shrine
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting Atsuta Jingu, a major shrine, follow the general shrine etiquette of bowing at the torii and purifying at the temizuya.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet in SCMAGLEV
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet and refrain from loud conversations when visiting the SCMAGLEV and Railway Park to respect other visitors enjoying the exhibits.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Navigating Osu Kannon Temple Grounds
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting Osu Kannon Temple, be mindful of worshippers and maintain a respectful distance during prayers or rituals.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Dogo Onsen Honkan Queue
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Be mindful of queues and follow staff guidance when choosing your bathing course at the historic Dogo Onsen Honkan, especially during busy periods.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Ryukyuan Sacred Sites
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Observe solemnity at places like Shikinaen Garden or Seifa Utaki, which hold deep historical and spiritual significance for Okinawa.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Greet Elders with Respect
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: In Okinawa, showing respect to elders (Uchinaanchu) is deeply valued. A polite bow and soft tone are always appreciated.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet on Botchan Train
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet demeanor on the Botchan Ressha, respecting the historic ambiance and other passengers during your ride.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Onsen Etiquette Near Sapporo
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting nearby onsen towns like Jozankei or Hoheikyo, always shower thoroughly before entering the communal baths and keep your towel out of the water.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Ritsurin Garden Paths
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Stay on designated gravel paths and do not touch the ancient pines or koi in Ritsurin Garden to preserve its meticulously maintained beauty.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Ferry Boarding Courtesy
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Board ferries to the Seto Inland Sea islands in an orderly manner, respecting queue lines and priority seating for those in need.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Snow Festival Sculptures
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: During the Sapporo Snow Festival, admire the intricate snow and ice sculptures without touching them, as this can cause damage.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Respect Sankeien Serenity
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet and avoid littering to preserve the tranquil atmosphere of Sankeien Garden, a historic Japanese landscape garden in Yokohama.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Mind Brewery Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting breweries like Kirin Beer Village in Yokohama, respect tasting guidelines and the tour environment for an enjoyable experience.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Solo Shrine Visits
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Shrines feel natural to visit alone. The prayer ritual is personal by design — bow, clap, make a wish. Nobody expects you to be with anyone.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Customize Your Sanuki Udon Bowl
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Sanuki Udon is served hot (atsu-atsu) or cold (hiya-hiya). Choose your desired temperature, add tempura or other toppings (tenpura) and condiments (like green onions).

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Department Store Greeting Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Staff at department stores (depato) bow and greet you at entrances and escalators. A nod back is polite but not required.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Theater and Kabuki Audience Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Arrive 15 minutes early, silence your phone completely, and never eat during performances. Kabuki at Kabuki-za in Ginza sells single-act tickets from ¥1,000.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Weekday Matinee Discounts
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Movie theaters, bowling alleys, and karaoke rooms offer steep weekday afternoon discounts. Cinema tickets drop from ¥1,900 to ¥1,300 on Ladies' Day (Wednesday at most chains).

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Gyudon Chains: Yoshinoya, Matsuya, and Sukiya
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: The big three gyudon chains serve beef bowls from ¥400-500. Yoshinoya is the original (est. 1899), Matsuya includes free miso soup, Sukiya has the most variety. All open 24/7 at most locations.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Slurping Is Expected and Encouraged
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Loud slurping aerates the broth and cools the noodles — it signals to the cook that you're enjoying the bowl. Don't hold back.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Use the Ticket Machine Before You Sit
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Most ramen shops use a ticket machine (shokkenki) at the entrance. Insert cash, press the button for your bowl, and hand the ticket to the cook.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Shoes Off at Traditional Izakayas
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Some traditional izakayas have tatami or raised seating areas where you remove shoes. Look for a shoe shelf (getabako) or sunken floor at the entrance as a cue.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: The Ramen Museum recreates a 1958 Tokyo streetscape with 6-8 shops serving regional ramen from across Japan. Buy mini portions (ミニ) to try multiple bowls.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Chain vs Local — Both Have Their Place
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Chains like Ichiran and Ippudo are consistent and tourist-friendly with English menus. But one-off neighborhood shops often serve the most memorable bowls.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Kitakata Ramen — Tohoku's Underrated Bowl
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Kitakata in Fukushima has the highest ramen shops per capita in Japan. The style features flat, thick curly noodles in a clean pork and niboshi (dried sardine) shoyu broth.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Soy Sauce Etiquette — Dip the Fish, Not the Rice
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Turn nigiri upside down and lightly dip the fish side into soy sauce. Dunking the rice soaks it up, overpowers the flavor, and causes the piece to fall apart.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Hakodate Morning Market — Squid Sashimi Alive
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Hakodate's Asaichi morning market opens at 5am. Try ika-odori-don — a live squid sashimi bowl where soy sauce makes the tentacles dance on your rice.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Nigiri, Chirashi, Temaki — Know the Formats
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Nigiri: fish on pressed rice. Chirashi: assorted sashimi scattered over a bowl of rice. Temaki: hand-rolled cone. Chirashi is often the best value for variety.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Omakase Budget — What to Expect
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Lunch omakase runs ¥10,000-15,000 at many respected counters — significantly cheaper than dinner (¥20,000-30,000+). Book 2-4 weeks ahead for popular spots.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Seasonal Specials — Ask for Shun no Mono
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Ask the chef "shun no mono wa nan desu ka?" (what's in season?) to get the day's best fish. This shows respect for the craft and often unlocks off-menu items.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Wabi-Sabi — Beauty in Imperfection
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Wabi-sabi finds beauty in age, wear, and asymmetry — a cracked tea bowl repaired with gold (kintsugi), moss on stone, weathered wood. Perfection isn't the goal; authenticity is.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Temple Accessibility
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Many temples require removing shoes and climbing wooden stairs inside. Some provide wheelchair-accessible viewing areas outside the main hall. Ask at the ticket window about accessible options.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Meishi — Business Card Exchange Ritual
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Business cards (meishi) are exchanged with both hands, a bow, and studied respectfully. Never write on, fold, or stack objects on a received meishi. It represents the person's identity.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Never Step on Wooden Thresholds
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: At temple gates and shrine halls, always step over the raised wooden threshold — never on it. Stepping on it is considered deeply disrespectful in both Buddhist and Shinto traditions.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Genkan — The Shoe Removal Ritual
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Remove shoes at the genkan (entrance area) of homes, ryokan, temples, and some restaurants. Step up from genkan to the elevated floor in socks or provided slippers. Separate toilet slippers exist i...

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Tea Ceremony Basics — Wa Kei Sei Jaku
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: The tea ceremony (sadō/chadō) is built on four principles: wa (harmony), kei (respect), sei (purity), jaku (tranquility). You don't need to know the rules perfectly — sincerity and attention are wh...

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Trail Etiquette — Carry Out All Trash
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Japanese hiking trails have no trash bins. Carry a bag and take all your garbage off the mountain. This includes food scraps, tissue, and packaging. Leave no trace — the mountains stay pristine bec...

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Responsible Wildlife Viewing in Nara
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Nara's 1,200+ deer are wild animals, not pets. Feed them only official shika senbei (deer crackers, ¥200) — human food causes illness. Don't chase, grab antlers, or corner them. Bowing triggers a b...

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Quiet Bar Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: High-end cocktail bars and whisky bars in Japan are quiet, focused spaces. Speak at low volume, don't take photos without asking, and appreciate the craft. These aren't places to get rowdy.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Sapporo: Weekend Subway Pass and Soup Curry
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Donichika subway day pass is ¥520 on weekends and holidays (vs ¥210-380 per ride). Soup curry (スープカレー) is Sapporo's signature meal — filling bowls from ¥800.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Handling Meltdowns with Empathy
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Japanese bystanders are understanding about crying children. A quick sumimasen and a bow goes far. Konbini snacks, train platform benches, and quiet shrine gardens are good reset spots.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Tanabata Festival Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: When visiting the Sendai Tanabata Festival in early August, admire the grand decorations but avoid touching them. Keep walkways clear for other visitors, especially in crowded shopping arcades.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Bar Etiquette: Volume and Behavior
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Japanese bars value a calm atmosphere. Keep your voice conversational. Don't shout across the room or between tables. Toasting (kanpai!) is fine — sustained rowdiness is not.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Tsuyu Is Not Constant Rain — Plan Around It
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Rainy season means intermittent rain, not continuous downpour. Waterproof shoes are the most important item. Many temples and gardens are more beautiful in the mist.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Street Photography Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Don't photograph geisha or maiko without permission — it's a persistent problem in Gion. Ask before photographing people, food stall vendors, or children. Respect privacy.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: On crowded trains, hold your backpack in front of you or put it on the luggage rack. Wearing it on your back takes up space and bumps people.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Phone vs Camera at Sacred Sites
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Using a phone camera is perceived as less intrusive than a large DSLR at temples and shrines. In prayer halls, even phones should be put away — show respect.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Solo Onsen Etiquette Made Simple
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Onsen solo is the best way to go — no coordination, just relaxation. Strip, wash thoroughly at seated showers, enter slowly. Small towel on head, not in water. Tattoo covers available at some spots.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Okinawa Beach Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Don't take coral or shells from beaches - it's illegal. Use reef-safe sunscreen. Jellyfish are common in summer - look for warning signs.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Never Stick Chopsticks Upright
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Placing chopsticks upright in rice resembles funeral incense rituals. Rest chopsticks on the holder or across your bowl when not eating.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette on Trains
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Remove backpacks and hold them in front of you or place them on overhead racks. Large bags take up space and bump other passengers.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Nagasaki Peace Park
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Like Hiroshima, maintain solemn respect at the Peace Park and Atomic Bomb Museum. The Peace Statue is for reflection, not casual selfies.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Don't Stare in Onsen
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Respect others' privacy in onsen. Avoid staring or making eye contact. The bathing experience is meant to be relaxing and respectful.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Bow at Torii Gates
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Bow once before passing through torii gates at shrines. The center of the path is reserved for the gods - walk along the sides.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Ask Before Photographing People
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Always ask permission before photographing individuals, especially in traditional dress like kimono or yukata. A bow and camera gesture works.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Koyasan Okunoin Cemetery
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Walk respectfully through Japan's largest cemetery. Don't step on graves, speak quietly, and don't disturb offerings. Most atmospheric at dusk or dawn.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Hanami Picnic Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: During cherry blossom season, don't shake branches, damage trees, or reserve spots overnight. Clean up completely after picnics.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Kenrokuen Garden Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Stay on designated paths, don't touch the famous Kotoji lantern, and don't feed the koi. Early morning visits (7 AM) offer peaceful experience.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Pour Drinks for Others
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: In group settings, pour drinks for others before yourself. Hold your glass with both hands when someone pours for you as a sign of respect.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Bowing on Phone is Normal
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Japanese people bow while talking on the phone - even though the other person can't see. It's unconscious and shows how ingrained bowing is. You'll catch yourself doing it!

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Mt. Fuji Viewing Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Fuji views depend on weather - often cloudy by afternoon. Best viewing is early morning. Respect private property when seeking view spots.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Fireworks Festival Etiquette
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: Arrive hours early for spots at popular hanabi (fireworks). Sit on provided tarps, don't stand blocking others. Many wear yukata. Clean up everything after.

etiquetteRelatedTips.title: Shrine Prayer Ritual
etiquetteRelatedTips.summary: At shrines: bow twice, clap twice, make your wish, then bow once more. This is the standard prayer ritual (nihai-nihakushu-ichihai).

transitRelatedTips.title: Accommodation Prices Peak in Kyoto
transitRelatedTips.summary: November Kyoto hotel rates are the year's highest. Budget hotels reach ¥20,000+/night. Stay in Osaka (30 min by train) for 40-60% savings.

transitRelatedTips.title: Priority Seating Respect
transitRelatedTips.summary: Priority seats near train doors are for elderly, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. Offer your seat when needed.

transitRelatedTips.title: Summer Heat on Platforms
transitRelatedTips.summary: Open-air platforms get very hot in summer (July-September). Underground stations and air-conditioned cars are a relief. Stay hydrated.

transitRelatedTips.title: Queue Lines on Platforms
transitRelatedTips.summary: Line up at marked waiting areas on train platforms. Let passengers exit before boarding. Don't rush or push.

transitRelatedTips.title: Quiet on Trains
transitRelatedTips.summary: Keep phone conversations and loud chatter to a minimum on trains. Set phones to silent/manner mode. Shinkansen has designated quiet cars.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use Both Hands for Exchanges
transitRelatedTips.summary: Present business cards, gifts, and money with both hands. Receive items the same way. Shows respect and gratitude.

transitRelatedTips.title: Umbrella Etiquette
transitRelatedTips.summary: Shake wet umbrellas outside and use provided umbrella bags or lockers. Don't bring dripping umbrellas into shops or trains.

transitRelatedTips.title: IC Card Convenience
transitRelatedTips.summary: Get a Suica or Pasmo card for seamless travel. Works on trains, buses, and even convenience stores. Saves time and avoids ticket hassles.

transitRelatedTips.title: Train Travel with Kids
transitRelatedTips.summary: Children under 6 ride free on trains. Ages 6–11 are half price. Reserve seats on shinkansen — the last car has extra legroom and is closest to the multi-purpose room for diaper changes.

transitRelatedTips.title: Choose Kintetsu Nara Station
transitRelatedTips.summary: For closer access to Nara Park, Todai-ji, and Kofuku-ji, use Kintetsu Nara Station, which is more central than JR Nara.

transitRelatedTips.title: Iya Valley Remoteness
transitRelatedTips.summary: Iya Valley is remote - rent a car or join a tour. Limited bus service exists but is infrequent. The vine bridges and scenery are worth the effort.

transitRelatedTips.title: Obon Travel Warning
transitRelatedTips.summary: Obon (mid-August) is peak domestic travel. Trains, flights, and hotels book up fast. Expect huge crowds at popular destinations. Book months ahead.

transitRelatedTips.title: Nursing and Diaper Rooms
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most department stores and large train stations have dedicated baby rooms (akachan no heya) with changing tables, hot water for formula, and private nursing spaces.

transitRelatedTips.title: Seven-Five-Three Festival
transitRelatedTips.summary: Shichi-Go-San (Nov 15) celebrates children aged 3, 5, and 7. Shrines will be busy with families in traditional dress. A lovely cultural sight!

transitRelatedTips.title: Hakone Busy Periods
transitRelatedTips.summary: Hakone is packed on weekends and holidays. Weekday visits are far more pleasant. The 'Hakone Free Pass' is worth it for multi-transport exploration.

transitRelatedTips.title: Utilize Tokyo Metro Day Passes
transitRelatedTips.summary: For heavy subway use in Tokyo, consider a 24, 48, or 72-hour Tokyo Metro ticket for unlimited rides within a set period.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ekiben Station Bento
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ekiben (駅弁) are special bento sold at train stations, often featuring local specialties. Eating ekiben on long-distance trains is a beloved tradition.

transitRelatedTips.title: Last Train Times
transitRelatedTips.summary: Last trains (終電 shuden) run around 11:30 PM - 12:30 AM depending on the line. Miss it and you're stuck until 5 AM. Check times for YOUR station, not terminus.

transitRelatedTips.title: Airport Luggage Delivery
transitRelatedTips.summary: Send luggage directly to/from airports with Yamato or Sagawa. Drop off 2 days before flight. Pick up at airport counter. No dragging bags on trains!

transitRelatedTips.title: Love Hotels Aren't Shady
transitRelatedTips.summary: Love hotels offer clean, themed rooms with great facilities. 'Rest' (2-4 hours) or 'Stay' (overnight) rates. Often cheaper than business hotels. Anyone can use them.

transitRelatedTips.title: Night Buses Save Money
transitRelatedTips.summary: Overnight highway buses between cities cost 1/3 of shinkansen fares. Not glamorous but practical. Book through Willer Express or Japan Bus Lines.

transitRelatedTips.title: Master the Tokyo Metro App
transitRelatedTips.summary: Download a reliable Tokyo train navigation app like "Japan Transit Planner" or Google Maps for real-time routes, fares, and platform numbers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Mind Rush Hour Traffic
transitRelatedTips.summary: Avoid Tokyo's intense morning (7-9 AM) and evening (5-7 PM) rush hours on major train lines if possible. Trains are packed.

transitRelatedTips.title: Navigate Station Labyrinths
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo's major stations like Shinjuku and Tokyo Station are huge multi-level complexes; follow signs carefully and use maps to avoid getting lost.

transitRelatedTips.title: Explore Free Observation Decks
transitRelatedTips.summary: Get stunning panoramic views of Tokyo for free at places like the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building in Shinjuku.

transitRelatedTips.title: Winter Snow Delays
transitRelatedTips.summary: Heavy snowfall in northern Japan and along the Sea of Japan coast can delay or cancel trains. The Shinkansen is surprisingly resilient.

transitRelatedTips.title: Reflect at the Earthquake Memorial
transitRelatedTips.summary: Visit the Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake Memorial Park to understand the devastation of the 1995 earthquake and Kobe's incredible recovery.

transitRelatedTips.title: Efficient Sightseeing with City Loop Bus
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize the Kobe City Loop Bus for convenient access to major tourist attractions like Kitano Ijinkan, Nankinmachi, and Meriken Park.

transitRelatedTips.title: Master Umeda's Underground Maze
transitRelatedTips.summary: Umeda Station's vast underground network can be confusing. Use station maps and look for colored lines or specific exit numbers for navigation.

transitRelatedTips.title: Maximize with Osaka Amazing Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: Consider purchasing the Osaka Amazing Pass for unlimited subway/bus rides and free entry to over 40 popular attractions like Osaka Castle.

transitRelatedTips.title: Savor Osaka's Street Takoyaki
transitRelatedTips.summary: Experience authentic takoyaki from bustling street stalls in areas like Dotonbori or Shinsekai; it's a quintessential Osaka food experience.

transitRelatedTips.title: Master the Kyoto City Bus
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize Kyoto's extensive bus network; purchase a One-Day Bus Pass (approx. 700 yen) for unlimited rides within the city zone, saving money.

transitRelatedTips.title: Suica Refund Before Leaving
transitRelatedTips.summary: Return your IC card at any JR ticket office before leaving Japan. You'll get back the ¥500 deposit plus remaining balance (minus ¥220 fee).

transitRelatedTips.title: Rent a Bicycle for Nara
transitRelatedTips.summary: Consider renting a bicycle near JR or Kintetsu Nara Station to easily navigate between sights beyond the main park.

transitRelatedTips.title: Check Winter Transport Advisories
transitRelatedTips.summary: Monitor weather forecasts for potential train (especially Shinkansen) and flight delays, particularly if traveling through snowy regions like Hokkaido, Tohoku, or the Japan Alps.

transitRelatedTips.title: Solo Safety in Japan
transitRelatedTips.summary: Japan is one of the safest countries for solo travelers. Trains run late, streets are well-lit, and convenience stores are 24/7. Trust your instincts but know the baseline risk is very low.

transitRelatedTips.title: Solo Travel by Train
transitRelatedTips.summary: Train travel in Japan is designed for individuals. Reserved seats mean guaranteed space. Platform markings show exactly where to stand. Announcements are in English on major lines.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use the Osaka Metro Day Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: For extensive inner-city travel, consider a 1-day Osaka Metro Pass for unlimited rides on subway lines, saving money compared to single tickets.

transitRelatedTips.title: Respect Bartender's Craft
transitRelatedTips.summary: Observe the bartender's skill without interruption. Don't demand quick service if they are busy crafting drinks.

transitRelatedTips.title: Savor Kaisendon Respectfully at Morning Market
transitRelatedTips.summary: When enjoying a fresh seafood bowl (kaisendon) at Hakodate Morning Market, eat quickly and avoid lingering, especially during busy hours.

transitRelatedTips.title: Enjoy Free Ohori Park Tranquility
transitRelatedTips.summary: Visit Ohori Park, a beautiful classical Japanese park with a large pond, for a free and relaxing escape from Fukuoka's city bustle.

transitRelatedTips.title: Maximize with Fukuoka Subway Day Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: Purchase a Fukuoka City Subway One-Day Pass for 640 yen (weekdays) or 520 yen (weekends/holidays) for unlimited rides, a cost-effective way to explore.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use the Uminonakamichi Park Shuttle
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize the shuttle bus or rental bicycles to efficiently navigate the vast Uminonakamichi Seaside Park, especially if traveling with family or limited time.

transitRelatedTips.title: Accessible Toilets
transitRelatedTips.summary: Multi-purpose toilets (tahyō toire) are in most train stations, department stores, and public facilities. They're spacious with grab bars, changing tables, and ostomate support. Look for the wheelc...

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Yaesu vs Marunouchi
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Station has two sides: Yaesu (east, modern, bus terminal) and Marunouchi (west, historic brick facade, Imperial Palace side). Know which you need.

transitRelatedTips.title: Consider JR Pass for Hakodate Access
transitRelatedTips.summary: If traveling to Hakodate from other major cities, a Japan Rail Pass or Hokkaido Rail Pass can offer significant savings compared to individual train tickets.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use the Meipuru~pu Bus
transitRelatedTips.summary: Navigate Hiroshima's main attractions efficiently using the "Meipuru~pu" (Maple Loop) sightseeing bus, included with the JR Pass.

transitRelatedTips.title: Mt. Inasa Night View
transitRelatedTips.summary: Plan your visit to Mt. Inasa in the late afternoon to catch sunset and the "million-dollar" night view. Use the ropeway or shuttle bus.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use Nagoya Subway Day Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: Purchase a "Donichi Eco Kippu" (Weekend Eco Ticket) for unlimited subway and city bus travel on weekends and holidays for ¥600.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kyoto Station Coin Lockers
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto Station has large lockers on B1 near the Shinkansen gate and near the Tourist Info Center. They fill up fast — arrive before 10 AM.

transitRelatedTips.title: Photograph Kanazawa Station's Gate
transitRelatedTips.summary: Capture the unique Tsuzumi-mon (Drum Gate) at Kanazawa Station, a modern architectural marvel, especially at night when lit up.

transitRelatedTips.title: Respect Dogo Onsen Honkan Queue
transitRelatedTips.summary: Be mindful of queues and follow staff guidance when choosing your bathing course at the historic Dogo Onsen Honkan, especially during busy periods.

transitRelatedTips.title: Master Matsuyama Tram Travel
transitRelatedTips.summary: Purchase an Iyo Railway (Iyotetsu) IC card for seamless travel on Matsuyama's tram and bus lines, simplifying fares and transfers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet on Botchan Train
transitRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet demeanor on the Botchan Ressha, respecting the historic ambiance and other passengers during your ride.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use Sapporo-Otaru Welcome Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: For cost-effective travel, consider the Sapporo-Otaru Welcome Pass (¥1700), offering one day of unlimited JR train travel between Sapporo and Otaru, plus a one-day subway pass for Sapporo.

transitRelatedTips.title: Master Sapporo's Subway
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize the clean and efficient Sapporo Subway for easy navigation around the city's main attractions, especially in winter. Purchase an IC card for seamless travel.

transitRelatedTips.title: Nagoya Station Accessibility
transitRelatedTips.summary: Nagoya Station is largely accessible with elevators and ramps, but check specific platforms for assistance, especially for Shinkansen transfers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Shinjuku Day Trip Lines
transitRelatedTips.summary: From Shinjuku: Odakyu Line to Hakone, Keio Line to Mt. Takao, JR Chuo to Koenji/Kichijoji. Each has its own terminal area within the station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Capture Susukino at Night
transitRelatedTips.summary: Head to Susukino, Sapporo's vibrant entertainment district, after dark to capture its neon-lit streets and bustling nightlife atmosphere.

transitRelatedTips.title: Free City Views at Aer or SS30
transitRelatedTips.summary: For a free panoramic view of Sendai, head to the observation decks at the top of Aer Building (31F) or SS30 Building (30F). Both are conveniently located near Sendai Station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Navigate Sendai's Subway System
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize Sendai's two subway lines, Namboku and Tozai, for quick travel across the city. Purchase an IC card like Suica or Pasmo at station ticket machines for seamless boarding.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ride the Loople Sendai Bus
transitRelatedTips.summary: Use the Loople Sendai sightseeing bus to efficiently visit major attractions like Sendai Castle Ruins, Zuihoden, and Osaki Hachimangu Shrine. A one-day pass costs ¥620.

transitRelatedTips.title: IC Cards Cannot Cross Regional Boundaries
transitRelatedTips.summary: You can't tap in at Tokyo and tap out at Osaka — IC cards only work within a single regional zone. Exit the system, buy a long-distance ticket, then use your card again for local transit at your destination.

transitRelatedTips.title: IC Card Refunds Are Region-Locked
transitRelatedTips.summary: Suica cards can only be refunded at JR East stations, ICOCA only at JR West. If you buy ICOCA in Osaka and try to return it in Tokyo, you can't get the ¥500 deposit back.

transitRelatedTips.title: Welcome Suica App for iPhone
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Welcome Suica Mobile App lets iPhone users set up a digital Suica before landing in Japan. No physical card needed, no airport queue — activate it on the plane.

transitRelatedTips.title: Hiking Solo in Japan
transitRelatedTips.summary: Popular trails are well-marked and busy enough that you're never truly alone. Carry water and check last bus times from trailheads — some rural buses stop running by 5 PM.

transitRelatedTips.title: Use Kotoden for City Travel
transitRelatedTips.summary: Utilize the Kotoden (Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad) for efficient travel within Takamatsu and to Kotohira. Purchase tickets or use an IC card.

transitRelatedTips.title: Explore Takamatsu by Bicycle
transitRelatedTips.summary: Rent a bicycle from spots like Takamatsu Port (around 500-1000 yen/day) or near major stations for flexible exploration of Ritsurin Garden and city sights.

transitRelatedTips.title: Board Unreserved Shinkansen from the Origin Station
transitRelatedTips.summary: For unreserved seats, board where the train starts empty — Tokyo, Shin-Osaka, or Hakata. Boarding midway at Shinagawa or Kyoto often means standing the entire ride.

transitRelatedTips.title: JR Pass Now Supports QR Code Gate Entry
transitRelatedTips.summary: JR Pass holders can book seats via the SmartEX app and use QR codes at selected ticket gates. No more queuing at counter windows for every reservation.

transitRelatedTips.title: Manga Cafes as Last-Train Fallback
transitRelatedTips.summary: Missed the last train? Manga cafes (manga kissa) offer private cubicles with reclining chairs, showers, and free drinks for ¥1,500-3,000 per night. Far cheaper than taxis or capsule hotels.

transitRelatedTips.title: Airport Limousine Buses Drop at Hotel Doors
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Airport Limousine Bus from Narita (¥3,200) stops directly at major hotels — no transfers, no dragging luggage through stations. Book online for discounts.

transitRelatedTips.title: JR Miyajima Ferry Is Free with JR Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: The JR Miyajima Ferry from Miyajimaguchi is covered by JR Pass — save ¥300 per crossing. The competing Matsudai ferry isn't covered but often has shorter queues.

transitRelatedTips.title: Rural Buses: Board Rear, Pay Front, Carry Coins
transitRelatedTips.summary: Outside cities, board from the rear door and take a numbered ticket. Pay the exact fare at the front when exiting. Many rural buses accept only cash — carry coins.

transitRelatedTips.title: IC Card for Vending Machines and Small Purchases
transitRelatedTips.summary: Charge your Suica or PASMO at any station kiosk and tap to pay at vending machines, konbini, and coin lockers.

transitRelatedTips.title: 100-Yen Shops for Travel Supplies
transitRelatedTips.summary: Daiso, Seria, and Can Do sell travel-sized toiletries, chopsticks, stationery, and souvenirs starting at ¥110.

transitRelatedTips.title: Last Train and Nightlife Timing
transitRelatedTips.summary: Last trains in Tokyo and Osaka run around 12:00-12:30am. Miss it and you're looking at a ¥3,000-8,000 taxi or a manga cafe until 5am.

transitRelatedTips.title: Free Entertainment: Observation Decks and Public Spaces
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building (Shinjuku) has a free 45th-floor observatory. Osaka's Umeda Sky Building garden is ¥1,500 but Abeno Harukas has a free lower deck.

transitRelatedTips.title: Reserve Coin Lockers with the SPACER App
transitRelatedTips.summary: Large lockers vanish by late morning at major stations during peak seasons. The SPACER app shows real-time availability and lets you reserve a locker before arriving.

transitRelatedTips.title: Overnight Buses Save a Hotel Night and Transit Cost
transitRelatedTips.summary: Overnight highway buses run Tokyo-Osaka from ¥3,000 and Tokyo-Kyoto from ¥2,500. Willer Express and JR Bus offer reclining seats and even flat-bed pods. Book early for best prices.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ekiben: Station Bento Box Art
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ekiben (駅弁, station bento) are regional specialty lunch boxes sold at train stations from ¥800-1,500. Each city has signature versions. Buying one for a shinkansen ride is a beloved Japanese ritual.

transitRelatedTips.title: Hakone Free Pass — One of Japan's Best Transit Deals
transitRelatedTips.summary: Covers all Hakone buses, cable cars, ropeways, the pirate ship cruise, and round-trip from Shinjuku. 2-day pass pays for itself in a single day trip. Buy at Shinjuku's Odakyu counter.

transitRelatedTips.title: Thank the Bus Driver When You Exit
transitRelatedTips.summary: Saying "arigatou gozaimashita" to the driver when stepping off the bus is a deeply rooted custom, especially in rural areas. A simple nod works too.

transitRelatedTips.title: T's TanTan — Vegan Ramen at Tokyo Station
transitRelatedTips.summary: T's TanTan in Tokyo Station's Keiyo Street serves fully vegan ramen from ¥920. The sesame tantanmen is rich and satisfying — you won't miss the pork broth.

transitRelatedTips.title: Last Trains Set the Closing Time
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most izakayas close at 11pm-midnight, timed to the last trains (around 12:00-12:30am). Friday and Saturday nights may run later. Order last call 30 minutes before close.

transitRelatedTips.title: Sendai's Signature Is Gyutan, Not Ramen
transitRelatedTips.summary: Charcoal-grilled aged beef tongue (gyutan) is Sendai's iconic dish — not widely known outside Japan. Restaurants near Sendai Station serve it as a set with barley rice and oxtail soup.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Has Three Rail Systems
transitRelatedTips.summary: JR (green logo, includes Yamanote), Tokyo Metro (blue M), and Toei (green leaf). Different companies = separate fares unless using IC card.

transitRelatedTips.title: Harajuku / Meiji-jingumae
transitRelatedTips.summary: JR Harajuku Station and Metro Meiji-jingumae Station both serve Takeshita Street and Meiji Shrine. They're essentially the same location.

transitRelatedTips.title: Miyajima Ropeway to Mount Misen
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Miyajima Ropeway (¥1,840 round trip) climbs to near the summit of Mt. Misen (535m). From the ropeway station, a 30-minute hike reaches the peak for panoramic Seto Inland Sea views.

transitRelatedTips.title: New Year Closures
transitRelatedTips.summary: Many businesses close Dec 31 - Jan 3. Restaurants, shops, and attractions have reduced hours. Plan accordingly and check specific venue schedules.

transitRelatedTips.title: Yamanote Line Loop
transitRelatedTips.summary: The JR Yamanote Line loops around central Tokyo hitting all major stations. If you're unsure, just ride the loop — you'll get there eventually.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kyoto Buses Are Primary
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto's sights are spread out and poorly connected by train. City buses are the main transit. Get a bus day pass (¥700) from the station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Accessible Train Travel
transitRelatedTips.summary: JR stations have platform elevators and wheelchair spaces on trains. Station staff assist with ramps at the gap between platform and train. Request help at the ticket gate — they'll radio ahead.

transitRelatedTips.title: Standing Sushi Bars for Quick Quality
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tachigui-zushi (standing sushi bars) near train stations and markets serve fresh nigiri at ¥150-300 per piece with no reservation needed. In and out in 20 minutes.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Metro Day Pass
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Metro 24-hour pass (¥600) covers all Metro lines. Worth it if you take 3+ Metro rides in a day. Buy at Metro ticket machines.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Ekiben
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Station has Japan's best ekiben (station bento) selection at Gransta and Ecute shops. Try regional specialties from across Japan.

transitRelatedTips.title: San'in Coast Scenic Railway
transitRelatedTips.summary: The JR San'in Line hugs the Sea of Japan coast from Tottori to Hagi with dramatic cliffs, fishing villages, and empty beaches. One of Japan's most scenic local train rides.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Coin Lockers
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Station has large coin lockers near the Shinkansen gates (B1 floor). For hard-to-find lockers, check the Coin Locker Navi app.

transitRelatedTips.title: Chugoku Region Transit Strategy
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Sanyo Shinkansen connects Hiroshima, Okayama, and Shin-Yamaguchi quickly. For San'in coast (Tottori, Matsue, Izumo), use JR limited express trains — connections are less frequent, plan around t...

transitRelatedTips.title: Tactile Paving Navigation
transitRelatedTips.summary: Yellow tactile paving lines guide visually impaired travelers through stations and sidewalks. Raised dots mean stop, raised bars mean follow. These are everywhere in urban Japan.

transitRelatedTips.title: Staying Out Past Last Train
transitRelatedTips.summary: Manga cafes and net cafes offer reclining seats, showers, and drinks from around ¥1,500 for an overnight stay. They're a practical backup if you miss last train — not just for emergencies.

transitRelatedTips.title: Meishi — Business Card Exchange Ritual
transitRelatedTips.summary: Business cards (meishi) are exchanged with both hands, a bow, and studied respectfully. Never write on, fold, or stack objects on a received meishi. It represents the person's identity.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Character Street
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Character Street (B1 Yaesu side) has official shops for Studio Ghibli, Pokemon, Hello Kitty, and more. Great for souvenirs between trains.

transitRelatedTips.title: Shinjuku Station Navigation
transitRelatedTips.summary: Shinjuku is the world's busiest station. Use the color-coded exit numbers. West exit = business district. East/South exit = Kabukicho and shopping.

transitRelatedTips.title: Onsen Water Conservation
transitRelatedTips.summary: Wash and rinse thoroughly at the shower station before entering the onsen pool. The bath water is shared by everyone — soap, shampoo, and body grime must stay out. One clean pool serves dozens of b...

transitRelatedTips.title: Ueno for Museums and Parks
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ueno Station (JR/Metro) exits directly to Ueno Park, the National Museum, and Ameya-Yokocho market. Park exit is on the west side.

transitRelatedTips.title: Shinjuku Bus Terminal (Busta)
transitRelatedTips.summary: Busta Shinjuku (南口/South Exit, 4F) is Tokyo's main highway bus terminal. Cheap overnight buses to Osaka, Kyoto, and beyond depart here.

transitRelatedTips.title: Buy Individually Wrapped Snacks as Omiyage Gifts
transitRelatedTips.summary: Japanese omiyage culture expects boxed treats (one piece per person) for colleagues and hosts. Each prefecture has signature sweets — buy at station kiosks for convenience.

transitRelatedTips.title: Winter Travel in Tohoku
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tohoku gets heavy snowfall November-March. Mountain areas can accumulate 2-3 meters. Pack insulated waterproof boots, layers, and hand warmers. Trains may be delayed by snow — allow buffer time.

transitRelatedTips.title: Shibuya Station Hachiko Exit
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Hachiko exit (ハチ公口) leads to the famous scramble crossing and statue. Use this exit for Shibuya Center-gai, 109, and most shopping.

transitRelatedTips.title: Typhoon Season Delays
transitRelatedTips.summary: September-October typhoons can halt trains for hours. Check weather forecasts and have backup plans. JR East app shows real-time disruptions.

transitRelatedTips.title: Discover Minoo Park's Fall Leaves
transitRelatedTips.summary: Visit Minoo Park during fall to witness stunning autumn foliage and enjoy a scenic hike to Minoo Waterfall, accessible by train from Umeda.

transitRelatedTips.title: Obon Week: August 13-16
transitRelatedTips.summary: Obon is Japan's Buddhist ancestral remembrance. Many businesses close August 13-16. Shinkansen and domestic flights are fully booked. Urban attractions thin out.

transitRelatedTips.title: Azalea (Tsutsuji) Gardens Peak
transitRelatedTips.summary: Azaleas blanket hillsides in brilliant reds and pinks during May. Nezu Shrine (Tokyo) has 3,000 azalea bushes. Tatebayashi in Gunma is another hotspot.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ueno to Narita Skyliner
transitRelatedTips.summary: Keisei Skyliner from Ueno to Narita Airport takes just 41 minutes (¥2,520). Much faster than the JR Narita Express from Tokyo Station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Nikko Peak Foliage: Early-to-Mid October
transitRelatedTips.summary: Nikko's Irohazaka Winding Road, Lake Chuzenji, and Ryuzu Falls hit peak color around October 10-20. A 2-hour day trip from Tokyo's Asakusa Station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ikebukuro East vs West
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ikebukuro East exit = Sunshine City, anime shops, restaurants. West exit = Tobu department store, Metropolitan Hotel. Don't mix them up.

transitRelatedTips.title: Fall Foliage Routes
transitRelatedTips.summary: Trains to Nikko, Hakone, and Kyoto get crowded during peak koyo (Nov). The Sagano Romantic Train and Hakone Tozan Railway offer scenic autumn views.

transitRelatedTips.title: New Year Rush Starts December 28
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most businesses close December 29-January 3 for oshogatsu (New Year). Department stores, banks, and museums shut down. Konbini and some restaurants stay open.

transitRelatedTips.title: Wheelchair Rental at Airports and Major Stations
transitRelatedTips.summary: Free wheelchair loans available at Narita, Haneda, Kansai, and all major JR stations. Ask at the service counter (midori no madoguchi) — no reservation needed for short-term use.

transitRelatedTips.title: Shinagawa Shinkansen Hub
transitRelatedTips.summary: Shinagawa is a Shinkansen stop closer to south Tokyo than Tokyo Station. Use it for Tokaido Shinkansen to Kyoto/Osaka if you're in Shibuya/Shinagawa area.

transitRelatedTips.title: Booking Accessible JR Pass Seats
transitRelatedTips.summary: Reserve wheelchair-accessible shinkansen seats at Midori no Madoguchi at least 2 days before travel. Online booking doesn't support accessible seat selection yet.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kyoto Station Orientation
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto Station: Shinkansen is on the south (Hachijo) side. Buses depart from the north (Karasuma) side. The station is a landmark building itself.

transitRelatedTips.title: Labor Thanksgiving Day: November 23
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kinro Kansha no Hi (Labor Thanksgiving Day) is a national holiday. Combined with the preceding weekend, it creates a busy 3-day stretch. Book ahead.

transitRelatedTips.title: Japan Rail Pass Value Calculation
transitRelatedTips.summary: A 7-day JR Pass costs ¥50,000. Tokyo→Kyoto round trip alone is ¥27,320. Add one more long-distance trip and the pass pays for itself. Use the JR Pass calculator online.

transitRelatedTips.title: Queue on Platform Markings
transitRelatedTips.summary: Line up at the marked spots on the platform — train doors align precisely. Two lines per door. Let passengers off before boarding.

transitRelatedTips.title: Family Restrooms with Changing Tables
transitRelatedTips.summary: Multi-function restrooms (tahō-men toire/多機能トイレ) in stations and malls have baby seats, changing tables, and child-size toilets. Look for the baby icon on signs.

transitRelatedTips.title: Stroller-Friendly Trains and Priority Spaces
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most JR and metro trains have wheelchair/stroller spaces near doors. Shinkansen cars 11-12 have extra legroom rows. Elevators exist at all major stations — follow the エレベーター signs.

transitRelatedTips.title: Service Animal Policies in Japan
transitRelatedTips.summary: Service dogs (hojoken/補助犬) are legally allowed in all public facilities, trains, restaurants, and hotels under the Assistance Dog Act. Carry documentation.

transitRelatedTips.title: Hearing Loops at Public Venues
transitRelatedTips.summary: Major museums, theaters, and train stations have hearing loop systems (磁気ループ). Look for the ear symbol with T. Activate T-coil mode on your hearing aid.

transitRelatedTips.title: Ostomate-Accessible Restrooms
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ostomate facilities (オストメイト) with specialized sinks are in most major stations, department stores, and service areas. Look for the ostomate pictogram on restroom signs.

transitRelatedTips.title: Braille on Vending Machines and Public Signage
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most vending machines have Braille labels on buttons. Train ticket machines have Braille on keypads. Tactile paving (tenshoku burokku) guides to platforms.

transitRelatedTips.title: Priority Seats on Trains
transitRelatedTips.summary: Priority seats (yūsen zaseki/優先座席) are marked in a different color near train doors. They're for elderly, disabled, pregnant, and injured passengers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Reading Slope and Ramp Signage
transitRelatedTips.summary: Look for スロープ (surōpu/slope), エレベーター (erebētā/elevator), and 段差なし (dansa nashi/no steps). These kanji appear on barrier-free route signs at stations.

transitRelatedTips.title: Station Elevator Navigation Apps
transitRelatedTips.summary: Download the JR East or Navitime app for step-free route planning. They show elevator locations, accessible gates, and platform gap sizes at each station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo: Toei Oedo Line Fully Accessible
transitRelatedTips.summary: The Toei Ōedo Line is Tokyo's most accessible subway — every station has elevators, wide gates, and platform doors. Covers Roppongi, Tsukiji, Shinjuku, Ueno-Okachimachi.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kyoto: Accessible Bus Kneeling Feature
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto city buses kneel (lower) at stops on request. Press the wheelchair button by the rear door or tell the driver. Ramps deploy automatically on newer buses.

transitRelatedTips.title: Osaka: Metro Elevator Coverage
transitRelatedTips.summary: Osaka Metro has elevators at all 133 stations. The Midosuji Line (red) is the most spacious for wheelchairs. Station staff will bridge platform gaps on request.

transitRelatedTips.title: Women-Only Cars
transitRelatedTips.summary: Some trains have women-only cars during rush hour (marked in pink). Men should avoid these cars during designated hours, typically weekday mornings.

transitRelatedTips.title: Naha: Kokusai Street and Monorail Accessibility
transitRelatedTips.summary: Kokusai Street (国際通り) is flat and wide — ideal for wheelchairs. The Yui Rail monorail has elevators at all 19 stations and level boarding with no platform gap.

transitRelatedTips.title: Sendai: Station Elevator Map and Zuihoden Access
transitRelatedTips.summary: Sendai Station provides printed barrier-free maps at the information desk showing all elevator and ramp locations. Zuihoden mausoleum has an accessible path to the main hall.

transitRelatedTips.title: Highway Buses vs Shinkansen
transitRelatedTips.summary: Willer Express and other highway buses run Tokyo→Osaka from ¥3,500 (vs ¥13,870 shinkansen). Night buses save a hotel night. Book on willerexpress.com or kosokubus.com.

transitRelatedTips.title: Coin Lockers at Stations
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー) in 3 sizes: small (¥400), medium (¥500), large (¥700). Pay with IC card or coins. 3-day max.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tokyo: 72-Hour Metro Pass and Free Observation Decks
transitRelatedTips.summary: Toei+Metro 72hr pass costs ¥1,500 (saves vs individual fares of ¥170-320/ride). Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building has free observation decks on the 45th floor — skip paid towers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Hiroshima: Free Peace Memorial and JR Ferry
transitRelatedTips.summary: Peace Memorial Museum is ¥200 (free with disability cert). The JR ferry to Miyajima is covered by your JR Pass — the competing private ferry is not. Save ¥360 round trip.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kyoto: Free Temples and Smart Transport
transitRelatedTips.summary: Fushimi Inari and Nishiki Market are free. IC card single fares are now cheaper than the bus day pass (¥700) for under 4 trips. Walk Gion and Higashiyama — everything is close.

transitRelatedTips.title: Osaka: Amazing Pass and Kushikatsu Deals
transitRelatedTips.summary: Osaka Amazing Pass (¥2,800/day) includes subway + 50 attractions (Osaka Castle, river cruise, Tsutenkaku Tower). Shinsekai's kushikatsu starts at ¥100 per stick — eat 10 sticks for ¥1,000.

transitRelatedTips.title: Sapporo: Weekend Subway Pass and Soup Curry
transitRelatedTips.summary: Donichika subway day pass is ¥520 on weekends and holidays (vs ¥210-380 per ride). Soup curry (スープカレー) is Sapporo's signature meal — filling bowls from ¥800.

transitRelatedTips.title: Nara: Mostly Free and Walkable
transitRelatedTips.summary: Most Nara temples charge ¥500-600, deer park is free, and everything is walkable from JR/Kintetsu Nara Station. Save on bus fare — the walk through the park is the experience.
