transitRelatedTips.title: Nagoya: 24-Hour Subway Pass and Underground Malls
transitRelatedTips.summary: Nagoya subway 24hr pass (donichi eco kippu) is ¥620 on weekends and holidays, ¥760 on weekdays. Underground shopping malls at Sakae and Meieki have budget food courts.

transitRelatedTips.title: Sendai: Gyutan Lunch Sets and Loople Bus
transitRelatedTips.summary: Gyutan (beef tongue) lunch sets start at ¥1,200 vs ¥2,500+ at dinner. Loople Sendai sightseeing bus day pass is ¥630 and covers Zuihoden, Aoba Castle site, and museums.

transitRelatedTips.title: Handling Meltdowns with Empathy
transitRelatedTips.summary: Japanese bystanders are understanding about crying children. A quick sumimasen and a bow goes far. Konbini snacks, train platform benches, and quiet shrine gardens are good reset spots.

transitRelatedTips.title: Family-Friendly Neighborhoods to Stay
transitRelatedTips.summary: Ueno (parks, zoo, museums), Odaiba (beaches, teamLab, malls), Namba (street food, arcades), Arashiyama (monkeys, bamboo, trains) — neighborhoods where families thrive without long commutes.

transitRelatedTips.title: Tachinomi Standing Bars for Budget Drinks
transitRelatedTips.summary: Tachinomi (立ち飲み/standing bars) serve drinks from ¥300 and small plates from ¥200 — no cover charge, no time limit. Found near stations. Perfect for solo travelers on a budget.

transitRelatedTips.title: Capsule Hotel as Last-Train Backup
transitRelatedTips.summary: Miss the last train? Walk-in capsule hotels near entertainment districts cost ¥3,000-4,500. No reservation needed. Many include a bath. Jalan.net shows same-night availability.

transitRelatedTips.title: Cash-Only vs Cashless Bars
transitRelatedTips.summary: Small bars (5-10 seats) are almost always cash-only. Larger bars and chains accept IC cards and credit cards. Carry at least ¥10,000 in cash for a night out. ATMs at konbini work 24/7.

transitRelatedTips.title: Yokohama: Noge Retro Bars and Harbor Views
transitRelatedTips.summary: Noge (野毛) near Sakuragichō Station is a retro bar district with 600+ tiny bars, tachinomi, and jazz clubs. Minato Mirai's rooftop bars offer harbor night views with cocktails from ¥800.

transitRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette
transitRelatedTips.summary: On crowded trains, hold your backpack in front of you or put it on the luggage rack. Wearing it on your back takes up space and bumps people.

transitRelatedTips.title: Burst Mode for Torii Gate Gaps
transitRelatedTips.summary: At busy torii gate tunnels (Fushimi Inari, Nezu Shrine), use burst mode and wait for 2-3 second gaps between groups. A 5-second burst gives you one clean empty frame.

transitRelatedTips.title: Kobe: Venus Bridge Night View
transitRelatedTips.summary: Venus Bridge (Vinasu Burijji) above Kitano gives a sweeping panorama of Kobe's harbor lights. Free access, 10-min walk uphill from Shin-Kobe Station. Bring a mini tripod.

transitRelatedTips.title: Keep Conversations Quiet
transitRelatedTips.summary: Loud conversations on trains are uncommon. Keep your voice low, especially on morning commutes. Headphones should not leak sound.

transitRelatedTips.title: Manga Kissa for Overnight Stays
transitRelatedTips.summary: Manga cafes (manga kissa/漫画喫茶) offer private booths with reclining seats, WiFi, drinks, and showers for ¥1,500-2,500 per night pack. Open 24/7. A budget fallback when you miss the last train.

transitRelatedTips.title: Day Trips That Work Perfectly Solo
transitRelatedTips.summary: Solo day trips from major cities require zero coordination. Kamakura, Nikko, Hakone from Tokyo. Nara, Himeji from Osaka/Kyoto. Just tap your IC card and go — no group logistics needed.

transitRelatedTips.title: Peak Typhoon Danger Is Late August Through September
transitRelatedTips.summary: Typhoons can halt trains, close attractions, and ground flights. Okinawa and Kyushu get hit worst. Check the JMA forecast 3-5 days ahead and have backup plans ready.

transitRelatedTips.title: Solo Female Traveler Tips
transitRelatedTips.summary: Japan is very safe for solo women. Women-only train cars run during rush hour. Female-only capsule hotels and hostel floors exist. Avoid touts in Kabukicho. Trust your instincts at izakaya.

transitRelatedTips.title: Carry Your Trash
transitRelatedTips.summary: Public trash cans are rare in Japan. Carry a small bag for your trash until you find a bin at a convenience store or train station.

transitRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette on Trains
transitRelatedTips.summary: Remove backpacks and hold them in front of you or place them on overhead racks. Large bags take up space and bump other passengers.

transitRelatedTips.title: Cash is Still King
transitRelatedTips.summary: Many small shops, restaurants, and traditional businesses are cash-only. Always carry ¥10,000-20,000 in cash. ATMs at convenience stores accept foreign cards.

transitRelatedTips.title: Cherry Blossom Packed Trains
transitRelatedTips.summary: Late March to mid-April: trains to popular hanami spots (Ueno, Meguro, Arashiyama) are extremely crowded on weekends. Go early morning.

transitRelatedTips.title: Off-Peak Benefits
transitRelatedTips.summary: Some passes offer off-peak discounts. Starting your day at 10 AM instead of 8 AM means cheaper fares and empty trains on some lines.

transitRelatedTips.title: Avoid Rush Hour
transitRelatedTips.summary: Weekday trains from 7:30-9:30 and 17:30-19:30 are extremely crowded. Travel before 7:00 or after 10:00 for a comfortable ride.

transitRelatedTips.title: Women-Only Train Cars
transitRelatedTips.summary: Many trains have women-only cars during rush hours (marked in pink). Men should not enter these cars during designated times.

transitRelatedTips.title: Free Shinkansen Reservations
transitRelatedTips.summary: With JR Pass, you can reserve shinkansen seats for free at JR ticket counters. Reserved cars are less crowded than non-reserved, especially during peak times.

transitRelatedTips.title: Less Crowded Lines Exist
transitRelatedTips.summary: Parallel routes are often less crowded. In Tokyo, Tokyo Metro is usually less packed than JR. Check alternatives on Google Maps.

transitRelatedTips.title: Speak Softly in Public
transitRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet voice in public spaces, especially on trains and in museums. Loud conversations are considered impolite.

transitRelatedTips.title: Coin Locker Strategy
transitRelatedTips.summary: Train stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー). Sizes range from ¥300-700. Large sizes fill up fast - arrive early. Some accept IC cards. Note locker number!

transitRelatedTips.title: Osaka Castle Park Crowds
transitRelatedTips.summary: Osaka Castle is busiest 10 AM - 3 PM. Visit early morning or late afternoon. The surrounding park is lovely for picnics but gets crowded during hanami.

transitRelatedTips.title: Obon Rush (Mid-August)
transitRelatedTips.summary: Obon (Aug 13-16) is a major travel period. Trains and Shinkansen fill up as people return to hometowns. Book early or enjoy emptier cities.

transitRelatedTips.title: Express vs Local Trains
transitRelatedTips.summary: Check train type before boarding. Express (急行) and rapid (快速) skip stations. Local (各停/各駅停車) stops everywhere. Wrong train = missed stop.

transitRelatedTips.title: New Year Transit Schedules
transitRelatedTips.summary: Dec 31 to Jan 3: many trains run all night on New Year's Eve, then reduced schedules Jan 1-3. Check your line's holiday timetable.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Accommodation Prices Peak in Kyoto
weatherRelatedTips.summary: November Kyoto hotel rates are the year's highest. Budget hotels reach ¥20,000+/night. Stay in Osaka (30 min by train) for 40-60% savings.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Priority Seating Respect
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Priority seats near train doors are for elderly, pregnant women, people with disabilities, and those with small children. Offer your seat when needed.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Summer Heat on Platforms
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Open-air platforms get very hot in summer (July-September). Underground stations and air-conditioned cars are a relief. Stay hydrated.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Queue Lines on Platforms
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Line up at marked waiting areas on train platforms. Let passengers exit before boarding. Don't rush or push.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Quiet on Trains
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Keep phone conversations and loud chatter to a minimum on trains. Set phones to silent/manner mode. Shinkansen has designated quiet cars.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Umbrella Etiquette
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Shake wet umbrellas outside and use provided umbrella bags or lockers. Don't bring dripping umbrellas into shops or trains.

weatherRelatedTips.title: IC Card Convenience
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Get a Suica or Pasmo card for seamless travel. Works on trains, buses, and even convenience stores. Saves time and avoids ticket hassles.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Train Travel with Kids
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Children under 6 ride free on trains. Ages 6–11 are half price. Reserve seats on shinkansen — the last car has extra legroom and is closest to the multi-purpose room for diaper changes.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Otaru Canal District
weatherRelatedTips.summary: The historic canal is beautiful but narrow paths get crowded. Visit at dusk when gas lamps light up. Winter illumination is magical but extremely cold.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Hydrangea Season
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Ajisai (hydrangeas) bloom during rainy season (June). Temples like Meigetsu-in in Kamakura are famous spots. Actually beautiful in the rain!

weatherRelatedTips.title: Obon Travel Warning
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Obon (mid-August) is peak domestic travel. Trains, flights, and hotels book up fast. Expect huge crowds at popular destinations. Book months ahead.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Nursing and Diaper Rooms
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Most department stores and large train stations have dedicated baby rooms (akachan no heya) with changing tables, hot water for formula, and private nursing spaces.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Ekiben Station Bento
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Ekiben (駅弁) are special bento sold at train stations, often featuring local specialties. Eating ekiben on long-distance trains is a beloved tradition.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Last Train Times
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Last trains (終電 shuden) run around 11:30 PM - 12:30 AM depending on the line. Miss it and you're stuck until 5 AM. Check times for YOUR station, not terminus.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Airport Luggage Delivery
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Send luggage directly to/from airports with Yamato or Sagawa. Drop off 2 days before flight. Pick up at airport counter. No dragging bags on trains!

weatherRelatedTips.title: Heated Toilet Seats
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japanese toilets have heated seats, bidets, and sound effects (to mask noise). Buttons are usually labeled in English. The big button is always flush.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Master the Tokyo Metro App
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Download a reliable Tokyo train navigation app like "Japan Transit Planner" or Google Maps for real-time routes, fares, and platform numbers.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Mind Rush Hour Traffic
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Avoid Tokyo's intense morning (7-9 AM) and evening (5-7 PM) rush hours on major train lines if possible. Trains are packed.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Limited Wheelchair Access
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Many shrines have limited wheelchair accessibility due to gravel paths, steps, or uneven terrain. Research ahead if mobility is a concern.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Avoid Flash Photography Indoors
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Refrain from using flash when photographing inside temple buildings and altars to protect artifacts.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Winter Snow Delays
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Heavy snowfall in northern Japan and along the Sea of Japan coast can delay or cancel trains. The Shinkansen is surprisingly resilient.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Aquariums for Rainy Days
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japan's aquariums are world-class and fully indoor. They make ideal rainy-day or hot-afternoon escapes with kids. Most have touch pools and feeding demonstrations.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Conserve Energy Indoors
weatherRelatedTips.summary: While buildings are well-heated, be mindful of excessive energy use. Turn off lights and AC when leaving rooms, and consider wearing an extra layer instead of high heating.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Be Mindful of Indoor Heating
weatherRelatedTips.summary: While it's cold outside, indoor heating can be intense. Adjust room temperatures responsibly and turn off lights/heating when leaving your accommodation to save energy.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Check Winter Transport Advisories
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Monitor weather forecasts for potential train (especially Shinkansen) and flight delays, particularly if traveling through snowy regions like Hokkaido, Tohoku, or the Japan Alps.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Solo Safety in Japan
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japan is one of the safest countries for solo travelers. Trains run late, streets are well-lit, and convenience stores are 24/7. Trust your instincts but know the baseline risk is very low.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Solo Travel by Train
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Train travel in Japan is designed for individuals. Reserved seats mean guaranteed space. Platform markings show exactly where to stand. Announcements are in English on major lines.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Eat Only in Designated Areas
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Refrain from walking and eating in most Japanese markets; look for specific eating spaces, benches, or stand-up counters provided by vendors.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Don't Touch Museum Exhibits
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Refrain from touching any museum exhibits, artworks, or display cases unless explicitly invited to do so by staff.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Check Mountain Weather Forecasts
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Before hiking in Japan's mountains, always check the local weather forecast, as conditions can change rapidly, especially at higher altitudes.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Onsen's Cultural Significance
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Appreciate hot springs (onsen) not just as baths, but as deeply ingrained cultural traditions for cleansing, healing, and community in Japan.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Photograph Dazaifu Tenmangu Respectfully
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Capture the beauty of Dazaifu Tenmangu Shrine's grounds and main hall exterior, but refrain from photography during active rituals or inside sacred inner areas.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Master Tenjin Underground City
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Utilize Fukuoka's extensive Tenjin Chikagai (Underground City) for efficient navigation, shopping, and dining, especially during bad weather.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Accessible Toilets
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Multi-purpose toilets (tahyō toire) are in most train stations, department stores, and public facilities. They're spacious with grab bars, changing tables, and ostomate support. Look for the wheelc...

weatherRelatedTips.title: Respect Kanazawa Chaya District Privacy
weatherRelatedTips.summary: When visiting Kanazawa's historic Chaya districts, refrain from disturbing residents or entering private properties, especially when geiko are present.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Consider JR Pass for Hakodate Access
weatherRelatedTips.summary: If traveling to Hakodate from other major cities, a Japan Rail Pass or Hokkaido Rail Pass can offer significant savings compared to individual train tickets.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet in SCMAGLEV
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Maintain quiet and refrain from loud conversations when visiting the SCMAGLEV and Railway Park to respect other visitors enjoying the exhibits.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Stay Hydrated and Sun Safe
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Naha's subtropical climate means strong sun and humidity. Drink plenty of water and use sunscreen to prevent heatstroke and sunburn.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Observe Quiet on Botchan Train
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet demeanor on the Botchan Ressha, respecting the historic ambiance and other passengers during your ride.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Use Sapporo-Otaru Welcome Pass
weatherRelatedTips.summary: For cost-effective travel, consider the Sapporo-Otaru Welcome Pass (¥1700), offering one day of unlimited JR train travel between Sapporo and Otaru, plus a one-day subway pass for Sapporo.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Prepare for Typhoon Season
weatherRelatedTips.summary: If traveling between May and October, be aware of typhoon season. Monitor weather forecasts closely as flights and ferries can be disrupted.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Explore Sendai's Shopping Arcades
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Wander through Sendai's extensive covered shopping arcades like Ichibancho and Clis Road for dining, shopping, and entertainment, especially convenient on rainy days.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Customize Your Sanuki Udon Bowl
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Sanuki Udon is served hot (atsu-atsu) or cold (hiya-hiya). Choose your desired temperature, add tempura or other toppings (tenpura) and condiments (like green onions).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Board Unreserved Shinkansen from the Origin Station
weatherRelatedTips.summary: For unreserved seats, board where the train starts empty — Tokyo, Shin-Osaka, or Hakata. Boarding midway at Shinagawa or Kyoto often means standing the entire ride.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Manga Cafes as Last-Train Fallback
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Missed the last train? Manga cafes (manga kissa) offer private cubicles with reclining chairs, showers, and free drinks for ¥1,500-3,000 per night. Far cheaper than taxis or capsule hotels.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Theater and Kabuki Audience Etiquette
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Arrive 15 minutes early, silence your phone completely, and never eat during performances. Kabuki at Kabuki-za in Ginza sells single-act tickets from ¥1,000.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Last Train and Nightlife Timing
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Last trains in Tokyo and Osaka run around 12:00-12:30am. Miss it and you're looking at a ¥3,000-8,000 taxi or a manga cafe until 5am.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Weekday Matinee Discounts
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Movie theaters, bowling alleys, and karaoke rooms offer steep weekday afternoon discounts. Cinema tickets drop from ¥1,900 to ¥1,300 on Ladies' Day (Wednesday at most chains).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Ekiben: Station Bento Box Art
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Ekiben (駅弁, station bento) are regional specialty lunch boxes sold at train stations from ¥800-1,500. Each city has signature versions. Buying one for a shinkansen ride is a beloved Japanese ritual.

weatherRelatedTips.title: How to Communicate Allergies in Japanese
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Say "[ingredient] arerugii ga arimasu" (I have a [__] allergy). Key terms: tamago (egg), ebi (shrimp), komugi (wheat), gyunyu (milk), soba (buckwheat), kani (crab).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Konbini Labels Help Identify Ingredients
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japanese convenience store food has detailed ingredient labels (原材料名). Learn key kanji: 卵 (egg), 乳 (dairy), 小麦 (wheat), えび (shrimp), 大豆 (soy), 肉 (meat).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Know the Essential Izakaya Dishes
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Core menu vocabulary: edamame (soybeans), karaage (fried chicken), yakitori (grilled skewers), sashimi, dashimaki tamago (egg roll), hiyayakko (cold tofu), tataki (seared).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Last Trains Set the Closing Time
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Most izakayas close at 11pm-midnight, timed to the last trains (around 12:00-12:30am). Friday and Saturday nights may run later. Order last call 30 minutes before close.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Rainy Season Preparation
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tsuyu (rainy season) runs mid-June to mid-July. Pack waterproof bags, quick-dry clothes, and a good umbrella. Indoor backup plans are essential.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Tsukemen — The Dipping Noodle Style
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tsukemen serves cold noodles and hot concentrated broth separately. Dip a few noodles at a time. Ask for soup-wari (broth dilution) at the end to drink the dip.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Kyoto Buses Are Primary
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Kyoto's sights are spread out and poorly connected by train. City buses are the main transit. Get a bus day pass (¥700) from the station.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Accessible Train Travel
weatherRelatedTips.summary: JR stations have platform elevators and wheelchair spaces on trains. Station staff assist with ramps at the gap between platform and train. Request help at the ticket gate — they'll radio ahead.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Standing Sushi Bars for Quick Quality
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tachigui-zushi (standing sushi bars) near train stations and markets serve fresh nigiri at ¥150-300 per piece with no reservation needed. In and out in 20 minutes.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Kairo Heat Packs
weatherRelatedTips.summary: In winter, buy kairo (カイロ) disposable heat packs at convenience stores. They last 12+ hours. Stick-on versions go inside clothes. Locals swear by them.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Wabi-Sabi — Beauty in Imperfection
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Wabi-sabi finds beauty in age, wear, and asymmetry — a cracked tea bowl repaired with gold (kintsugi), moss on stone, weathered wood. Perfection isn't the goal; authenticity is.

weatherRelatedTips.title: San'in Coast Scenic Railway
weatherRelatedTips.summary: The JR San'in Line hugs the Sea of Japan coast from Tottori to Hagi with dramatic cliffs, fishing villages, and empty beaches. One of Japan's most scenic local train rides.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Chugoku Region Transit Strategy
weatherRelatedTips.summary: The Sanyo Shinkansen connects Hiroshima, Okayama, and Shin-Yamaguchi quickly. For San'in coast (Tottori, Matsue, Izumo), use JR limited express trains — connections are less frequent, plan around t...

weatherRelatedTips.title: Staying Out Past Last Train
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Manga cafes and net cafes offer reclining seats, showers, and drinks from around ¥1,500 for an overnight stay. They're a practical backup if you miss last train — not just for emergencies.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Dress in Warm Layers
weatherRelatedTips.summary: January in Japan brings cold temperatures; pack thermal underwear, thick coats, hats, and gloves, especially if visiting northern regions or mountains.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Tokyo Station Character Street
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo Character Street (B1 Yaesu side) has official shops for Studio Ghibli, Pokemon, Hello Kitty, and more. Great for souvenirs between trains.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Winter Travel in Tohoku
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tohoku gets heavy snowfall November-March. Mountain areas can accumulate 2-3 meters. Pack insulated waterproof boots, layers, and hand warmers. Trains may be delayed by snow — allow buffer time.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Post-Golden Week Deals: May 7 Onward
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Hotel and flight prices drop sharply after May 6. The week of May 7-14 is one of Japan's best-value travel windows with perfect weather and thin crowds.

weatherRelatedTips.title: May Weather: T-Shirt Days Arrive
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka hit 22-26°C in May with low humidity. The most comfortable month for walking. UV is strong, so pack sunscreen and a hat for temple circuits.

weatherRelatedTips.title: October Weather: Japan's Best Month
weatherRelatedTips.summary: October offers 18-24°C daytime temps, low humidity, clear skies, and minimal rain. Pack light layers and one warmer piece for evenings.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Tsuyu Ends Late July
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Rainy season typically ends in Kanto/Kansai around July 19-21. The transition to full summer is abrupt: expect 30-33°C and high humidity within days.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Pack for Cold, Dry Winter Air
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Bring warm layers, including thermal underwear, waterproof outer shell, gloves, scarves, and warm hats. February can be very cold and dry, especially in northern regions.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Beat the Heat: Morning Temple Strategy
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Start sightseeing by 7-8am when temples open and temps are 24-26°C. By noon it hits 32°C+. Reserve afternoons for indoor museums, malls, or rest.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Typhoon Season Delays
weatherRelatedTips.summary: September-October typhoons can halt trains for hours. Check weather forecasts and have backup plans. JR East app shows real-time disruptions.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Discover Minoo Park's Fall Leaves
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Visit Minoo Park during fall to witness stunning autumn foliage and enjoy a scenic hike to Minoo Waterfall, accessible by train from Umeda.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Peak Summer Heat: 33-38°C Daily
weatherRelatedTips.summary: August is Japan's hottest month. Tokyo and Osaka regularly hit 35°C+ with oppressive humidity. Limit outdoor exposure between 11am-3pm.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Hokkaido Escapes the Rain
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Hokkaido has no tsuyu season. June offers 20-25°C weather, lavender fields starting to bloom, and none of the humidity that blankets Honshu.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Indoor Itinerary Backup Plans
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Rainy days are perfect for museums, depachika food halls, covered shotengai, onsen, and cooking classes. Don't waste a dry day on indoor activities.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Umbrella Essentials
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Buy a transparent vinyl umbrella at any konbini for ¥500-700. For longer trips, invest in a folding umbrella from Uniqlo or Muji (¥1,500-2,500).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Moss Gardens Peak in the Rain
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japan's famous moss gardens reach their most vivid green during tsuyu. Saihoji (Kyoto, ¥3,000 reservation required), Gioji, and Sanzenin are spectacular in rain.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Seasonal Dining: Ayu and Cold Noodles
weatherRelatedTips.summary: June introduces ayu (sweetfish) grilled on sticks at riverside restaurants, hiyashi chuka (cold ramen), and zaru soba (chilled buckwheat noodles).

weatherRelatedTips.title: Hydration Is Non-Negotiable
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Carry water constantly. Vending machines every 200 meters sell cold drinks for ¥110-160. Look for Pocari Sweat or Aquarius for electrolyte replacement.

weatherRelatedTips.title: November Weather: Comfortable but Cooling
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka: 12-18°C daytime, 6-10°C evenings. Kyoto's temples in mountain areas drop colder. Pack a proper jacket and scarf for evening koyo viewing.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Fall Foliage Routes
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Trains to Nikko, Hakone, and Kyoto get crowded during peak koyo (Nov). The Sagano Romantic Train and Hakone Tozan Railway offer scenic autumn views.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Typhoon Awareness Begins
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Typhoon season ramps up in August. Track storms on weather apps 3-5 days out. Flights and ferries cancel; shinkansen may delay. Have flexible backup plans.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Verify Winter Operating Hours
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Some outdoor attractions, gardens, or smaller museums may have reduced operating hours or closures during the colder, darker days of January. Always check ahead online.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Heat Lingers Through Mid-September
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Early September still hits 30-33°C in Tokyo and Osaka. The summer heat doesn't truly break until the autumn equinox around September 22-23.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Typhoon Season Peaks in September
weatherRelatedTips.summary: September is Japan's most active typhoon month. 2-3 typhoons typically approach or make landfall. Monitor forecasts daily and keep plans flexible.

weatherRelatedTips.title: September Hotel Deals Continue
weatherRelatedTips.summary: September remains low season for inbound tourism. Hotel prices stay 20-30% below peak across most cities. Typhoon risk keeps crowds down.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Okinawa Still in Summer Mode
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Okinawa stays 28-31°C through September with warm ocean water (27°C). Fewer tourists than July-August but typhoon risk is highest. Great diving conditions between storms.

weatherRelatedTips.title: December Weather: Cold but Clear
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka: 5-12°C daytime, near freezing at night. Kyoto drops to 2-8°C. Hokkaido is fully snowy (-5 to 2°C). Pack proper winter gear.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Queue on Platform Markings
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Line up at the marked spots on the platform — train doors align precisely. Two lines per door. Let passengers off before boarding.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Stroller-Friendly Trains and Priority Spaces
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Most JR and metro trains have wheelchair/stroller spaces near doors. Shinkansen cars 11-12 have extra legroom rows. Elevators exist at all major stations — follow the エレベーター signs.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Service Animal Policies in Japan
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Service dogs (hojoken/補助犬) are legally allowed in all public facilities, trains, restaurants, and hotels under the Assistance Dog Act. Carry documentation.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Hearing Loops at Public Venues
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Major museums, theaters, and train stations have hearing loop systems (磁気ループ). Look for the ear symbol with T. Activate T-coil mode on your hearing aid.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Braille on Vending Machines and Public Signage
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Most vending machines have Braille labels on buttons. Train ticket machines have Braille on keypads. Tactile paving (tenshoku burokku) guides to platforms.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Priority Seats on Trains
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Priority seats (yūsen zaseki/優先座席) are marked in a different color near train doors. They're for elderly, disabled, pregnant, and injured passengers.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Women-Only Cars
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Some trains have women-only cars during rush hour (marked in pink). Men should avoid these cars during designated hours, typically weekday mornings.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Vending Machine Drinks vs Cafes
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Vending machine drinks cost ¥120-160 (hot or cold). Cafe drinks start at ¥400-500+. With vending machines on every block, you rarely need to pay cafe prices for caffeine.

weatherRelatedTips.title: 2026 Cherry Blossoms Are Predicted Earlier Than Average
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Tokyo: peak ~March 28. Kyoto/Osaka: peak ~March 31. Sapporo: peak ~April 27. Dates shift 5-7 days based on winter temperatures. Follow JMC forecasts starting January.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Handling Meltdowns with Empathy
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japanese bystanders are understanding about crying children. A quick sumimasen and a bow goes far. Konbini snacks, train platform benches, and quiet shrine gardens are good reset spots.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Dress Warm for Sapporo Winters
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Sapporo winters are severely cold and snowy; pack heavy winter clothing, including waterproof jackets, thermal layers, hats, gloves, and sturdy, non-slip boots.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Family-Friendly Neighborhoods to Stay
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Ueno (parks, zoo, museums), Odaiba (beaches, teamLab, malls), Namba (street food, arcades), Arashiyama (monkeys, bamboo, trains) — neighborhoods where families thrive without long commutes.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Capsule Hotel as Last-Train Backup
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Miss the last train? Walk-in capsule hotels near entertainment districts cost ¥3,000-4,500. No reservation needed. Many include a bath. Jalan.net shows same-night availability.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Hokkaido Skips Rainy Season Entirely
weatherRelatedTips.summary: While the rest of Japan endures tsuyu from early June to mid-July, Hokkaido has significantly less rain and lower humidity. It's the best June destination in Japan.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Okinawa's Rainy Season Starts and Ends a Month Earlier
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Okinawa enters tsuyu in mid-May and exits by late June — a full month before the mainland. Visit Okinawa in July for post-rain beach weather before typhoon season peaks.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Tsuyu Is Not Constant Rain — Plan Around It
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Rainy season means intermittent rain, not continuous downpour. Waterproof shoes are the most important item. Many temples and gardens are more beautiful in the mist.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette
weatherRelatedTips.summary: On crowded trains, hold your backpack in front of you or put it on the luggage rack. Wearing it on your back takes up space and bumps people.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Umbrellas as Photography Props in Rain
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Rainy days create stunning reflections on wet streets and diffused light. A transparent vinyl umbrella (¥500 at any konbini) makes a beautiful photo prop against neon or temple backdrops.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Best Reflections After Rain
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Wet stone paths at temples, puddles on city streets, and rain-slicked crosswalks create perfect reflections. Shoot low — get your phone or camera near ground level for maximum effect.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Keep Conversations Quiet
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Loud conversations on trains are uncommon. Keep your voice low, especially on morning commutes. Headphones should not leak sound.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Manga Kissa for Overnight Stays
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Manga cafes (manga kissa/漫画喫茶) offer private booths with reclining seats, WiFi, drinks, and showers for ¥1,500-2,500 per night pack. Open 24/7. A budget fallback when you miss the last train.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Peak Typhoon Danger Is Late August Through September
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Typhoons can halt trains, close attractions, and ground flights. Okinawa and Kyushu get hit worst. Check the JMA forecast 3-5 days ahead and have backup plans ready.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Free Mugicha at Restaurants in Summer
weatherRelatedTips.summary: During summer, many restaurants serve free mugicha (麦茶, roasted barley tea) instead of water. It's caffeine-free, slightly nutty, and served ice-cold.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Solo Female Traveler Tips
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japan is very safe for solo women. Women-only train cars run during rush hour. Female-only capsule hotels and hostel floors exist. Avoid touts in Kabukicho. Trust your instincts at izakaya.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Carry Your Trash
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Public trash cans are rare in Japan. Carry a small bag for your trash until you find a bin at a convenience store or train station.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Backpack Etiquette on Trains
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Remove backpacks and hold them in front of you or place them on overhead racks. Large bags take up space and bump other passengers.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Cherry Blossom Packed Trains
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Late March to mid-April: trains to popular hanami spots (Ueno, Meguro, Arashiyama) are extremely crowded on weekends. Go early morning.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Off-Peak Benefits
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Some passes offer off-peak discounts. Starting your day at 10 AM instead of 8 AM means cheaper fares and empty trains on some lines.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Avoid Rush Hour
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Weekday trains from 7:30-9:30 and 17:30-19:30 are extremely crowded. Travel before 7:00 or after 10:00 for a comfortable ride.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Women-Only Train Cars
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Many trains have women-only cars during rush hours (marked in pink). Men should not enter these cars during designated times.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Speak Softly in Public
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Maintain a quiet voice in public spaces, especially on trains and in museums. Loud conversations are considered impolite.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Yozakura Night Viewing
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Many parks illuminate cherry blossoms at night (yozakura). Bring warm clothes - spring nights are cold. Food stalls often set up for evening crowds.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Coin Locker Strategy
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Train stations have coin lockers (コインロッカー). Sizes range from ¥300-700. Large sizes fill up fast - arrive early. Some accept IC cards. Note locker number!

weatherRelatedTips.title: Obon Rush (Mid-August)
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Obon (Aug 13-16) is a major travel period. Trains and Shinkansen fill up as people return to hometowns. Book early or enjoy emptier cities.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Express vs Local Trains
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Check train type before boarding. Express (急行) and rapid (快速) skip stations. Local (各停/各駅停車) stops everywhere. Wrong train = missed stop.

weatherRelatedTips.title: New Year Transit Schedules
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Dec 31 to Jan 3: many trains run all night on New Year's Eve, then reduced schedules Jan 1-3. Check your line's holiday timetable.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Japanese OTC Medicines Are Milder Than Western Equivalents
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Pain relievers, cold medicine, and allergy pills may feel weaker than what you're used to. Matsumoto Kiyoshi pharmacists can help select options even with limited English.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Summer Heat Precautions
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japanese summers are hot and humid (35°C+). Carry a hand towel, stay hydrated, and use cooling products from convenience stores. Heat stroke is real.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Bowing on Phone is Normal
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Japanese people bow while talking on the phone - even though the other person can't see. It's unconscious and shows how ingrained bowing is. You'll catch yourself doing it!

weatherRelatedTips.title: Mt. Fuji Viewing Etiquette
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Fuji views depend on weather - often cloudy by afternoon. Best viewing is early morning. Respect private property when seeking view spots.

weatherRelatedTips.title: Pseudoephedrine (Sudafed, Vicks Inhaler) Is BANNED in Japan
weatherRelatedTips.summary: Pseudoephedrine is classified as a stimulant raw material. Possession can lead to arrest. Leave Sudafed, Actifed, and Vicks nasal inhalers at home — buy Japanese cold medicine instead.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Summer Heat on Platforms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Open-air platforms get very hot in summer (July-September). Underground stations and air-conditioned cars are a relief. Stay hydrated.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Cherry Blossom Photography Tips
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Shoot sakura during golden hour or overcast skies to avoid blown-out whites. Shinjuku Gyoen (¥500) bans tripods. Meguro River is best at night with lanterns.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Summer Festival Behavior
seasonalFoodTips.summary: At summer matsuri (festivals), follow crowd flow, don't block paths for photos, and dispose of food waste at designated spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Firefly Season in Rural Japan
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Wild fireflies (hotaru) appear along streams from early-to-mid June. Uji (south of Kyoto), Shimanto River (Shikoku), and Genji Firefly Festival are top viewing spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Fresh Green Season (Shin-Ryoku)
seasonalFoodTips.summary: May's fresh green foliage (shin-ryoku) makes temples and mountain trails glow. Nikko, Kamakura, and Kyoto's eastern hills are stunning without sakura crowds.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Shirakawa-go Etiquette
seasonalFoodTips.summary: These are real homes - don't enter private property or peer into windows. Stay on designated paths. Winter illumination requires lottery reservation.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Otaru Canal District
seasonalFoodTips.summary: The historic canal is beautiful but narrow paths get crowded. Visit at dusk when gas lamps light up. Winter illumination is magical but extremely cold.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hydrangea Season
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Ajisai (hydrangeas) bloom during rainy season (June). Temples like Meigetsu-in in Kamakura are famous spots. Actually beautiful in the rain!

seasonalFoodTips.title: Bon Odori Participation
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Anyone can join bon odori (summer festival dances). Follow the simple steps - locals will help. Wearing yukata adds to the experience but isn't required.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Night Illuminations
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Autumn and winter bring spectacular light-up events at temples and gardens. Check schedules - many require advance tickets. Photography is often allowed.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Peak Sakura in Tokyo and Kansai
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Full bloom (mankai) hits Tokyo around March 28-April 2, Kyoto/Osaka April 2-8. Petals scatter within a week. Check daily forecasts to time your visit.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Sales Seasons
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Major sales happen in January (New Year) and July (summer). Department stores and fashion brands slash prices 30-70%. Mark your calendar if shopping is your goal.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Public Green Space Conduct
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Enjoy Tokyo's parks responsibly. Don't leave trash, avoid loud activities, and respect designated areas, especially during peak seasons like cherry blossom.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Winter Snow Delays
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Heavy snowfall in northern Japan and along the Sea of Japan coast can delay or cancel trains. The Shinkansen is surprisingly resilient.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Try Kyoto's Obanzai Cuisine
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Explore Obanzai, traditional Kyoto home-style dishes made with seasonal, local ingredients, often served in small, delectable portions.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Find Early Plum Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: While cherry blossoms are months away, late January marks the beginning of the plum blossom (ume) season in warmer areas like Atami and Fukuoka, signaling spring's approach.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Find Winter Travel Deals
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Mid-to-late January often presents opportunities for cheaper flights and accommodation as demand drops after the New Year holiday period, especially for domestic travel.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Savor Winter Nabe Dishes
seasonalFoodTips.summary: January is perfect for enjoying various hot pot (nabe) dishes, from chicken mizutaki to chankonabe (sumo wrestler's stew). Many restaurants offer special winter nabe menus.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hike Mt. Wakakusa for Fall Leaves
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Mt. Wakakusa in late fall for stunning panoramic views of Nara city and vibrant autumn foliage.

seasonalFoodTips.title: View Nara's Cherry Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience stunning cherry blossoms in Nara Park and particularly at Mt. Yoshino, a UNESCO site, during spring.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Shop End-of-Winter Sales
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Take advantage of extensive post-New Year and end-of-winter sales (hatsu-uri) continuing into February. Look for discounts on clothing, electronics, and household goods.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Warm Up with Hot Pot (Nabe)
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Indulge in various hot pot dishes (nabe) perfect for warming up in winter. Try Chanko Nabe (sumo wrestler stew) or seafood nabe with fresh seasonal ingredients.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Feast on Winter Seafood Delicacies
seasonalFoodTips.summary: February is prime season for delicious seafood like snow crab (kani) in Hokkaido or Ishikawa, and plump oysters (kaki) from Hiroshima. Don't miss these seasonal treats.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Check Winter Transport Advisories
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Monitor weather forecasts for potential train (especially Shinkansen) and flight delays, particularly if traveling through snowy regions like Hokkaido, Tohoku, or the Japan Alps.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Osaka's Winter Illuminations
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience the magical winter illuminations across Osaka from late November to December, with major displays in Umeda and Nakanoshima.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Focus on Seasonal Specialties
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Markets are prime places to discover and enjoy seasonal Japanese produce, seafood, and prepared foods that reflect the current time of year.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Secure Wellness Bookings Early
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Popular wellness retreats, temple stays (shukubo), and specialized onsen can book up months in advance, especially during peak seasons.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Capture Views from Fukuoka Castle Ruins
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit the Fukuoka Castle Ruins in Maizuru Park for panoramic views of the city skyline, especially during cherry blossom season in spring.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Witness Kenrokuen's Winter Yukitsuri
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience Kenrokuen Garden in winter (December-February) to see "yukitsuri," traditional rope supports protecting trees from heavy snow.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Savor Kanazawa's Jibuni Stew
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Don't miss trying Jibuni, a unique duck stew thickened with flour and often featuring seasonal vegetables, a true Kanazawa specialty.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Free Exploration at Goryokaku Park
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Explore the vast, star-shaped Goryokaku Park for free, a beautiful spot for a stroll, cherry blossom viewing, or simply relaxing.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Witness Goryokaku Park's Fall Foliage
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience stunning autumn colors at Goryokaku Park, typically from late October to early November, offering beautiful views from the tower and within the park.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Miyajima Oysters
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Indulge in fresh oysters, a local delicacy on Miyajima Island, especially delicious when in season during fall and winter.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Glover Garden Photo Opportunities
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Capture stunning photos at Glover Garden, featuring historic Western-style residences, panoramic harbor views, and seasonal flowers.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Photograph Odori Park Seasons
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Capture the beauty of Odori Park, a central green space that transforms with each season, from spring lilacs to winter snow sculptures.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Master Sapporo's Subway
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Utilize the clean and efficient Sapporo Subway for easy navigation around the city's main attractions, especially in winter. Purchase an IC card for seamless travel.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Try Nagoya's Tebasaki
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Indulge in Nagoya's famous Tebasaki (手羽先), crispy, seasoned chicken wings often enjoyed with a beer at izakayas.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Catch Naha's Early Cherry Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience Japan's earliest cherry blossom season in Naha, typically from mid-January to early February, long before mainland Japan.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Prepare for Typhoon Season
seasonalFoodTips.summary: If traveling between May and October, be aware of typhoon season. Monitor weather forecasts closely as flights and ferries can be disrupted.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Navigate Sapporo's Underground Passages
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Utilize the extensive underground pedestrian walkways (Chikagai) like Sapporo Ekimae-dori Chikaho and Aurora Town to stay warm and dry in winter.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Jozenji-dori Illuminations
seasonalFoodTips.summary: During winter, Jozenji-dori Avenue transforms with beautiful light illuminations, creating a magical atmosphere. This event typically runs from mid-December to early January.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Visit Sendai Morning Market
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience the lively atmosphere and fresh local produce at Sendai Morning Market (Sendai Asaichi), a great place to find seasonal fruits, vegetables, and seafood.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Autumn Leaves Viewing
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Popular koyo (autumn leaves) spots get extremely crowded. Visit early morning or late afternoon. Don't pick or damage leaves.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Mask Wearing
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Many Japanese wear masks when sick, during allergy season, or for privacy. Having a mask is useful and won't attract stares.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Reserve Coin Lockers with the SPACER App
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Large lockers vanish by late morning at major stations during peak seasons. The SPACER app shows real-time availability and lets you reserve a locker before arriving.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Mos Burger: Japan's Gourmet Fast Food
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Mos Burger uses fresh ingredients with made-to-order burgers from ¥390. The rice burger (ライスバーガー) replaces buns with grilled rice patties. Seasonal items rotate quarterly.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Vegetarian Dining Is Tricky — Dashi Is Everywhere
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Most Japanese 'vegetable' dishes use dashi stock made from bonito (fish flakes). Even miso soup, simmered vegetables, and rice seasonings often contain fish-based dashi.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Lawson Has the Best Bakery Aisle
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Lawson's bread and pastry selection is the largest among konbini chains. The viral Mochi Shokkan Roll (chewy mochi-cake with cream) is a must-try. Seasonal flavors rotate weekly.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hot Canned Coffee from Vending Machines
seasonalFoodTips.summary: In winter, vending machines sell hot canned coffee (marked with a red label). Boss and Georgia are the main brands. A hot can costs ¥120-150 and warms your hands too.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Konbini Limited Editions Vanish in Days
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Seasonal konbini items (sakura mochi in spring, roasted sweet potato treats in autumn, chocolate fondant in winter) appear and disappear within a week. Check the 'new items' shelf daily.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Rainy Season Preparation
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Tsuyu (rainy season) runs mid-June to mid-July. Pack waterproof bags, quick-dry clothes, and a good umbrella. Indoor backup plans are essential.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Fish — Eat What's at Peak
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Japanese sushi follows nature's calendar. Spring: tai (sea bream). Summer: aji (horse mackerel). Fall: sanma (pacific saury). Winter: buri (yellowtail) and fatty tuna.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Winter Is Peak Season for Snow Crab and Oysters
seasonalFoodTips.summary: December-February: Hokkaido snow crab, Hiroshima oysters (festival in February), and fugu in Osaka are all at their peak. Hot pot (nabe) is the quintessential winter dining experience.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Spring Katsuo (Bonito) Is Lean and Clean
seasonalFoodTips.summary: First-catch bonito (hatsu-gatsuo) arrives April-May — leaner and cleaner than autumn's fatty return catch. Tataki preparation (seared outside, raw inside) is the classic way to eat it.

seasonalFoodTips.title: End of Ski Season Deals in Hokkaido
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Hokkaido ski resorts like Niseko and Furano offer late-season discounts in March. Snow quality remains excellent with fewer crowds than January-February.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Autumn Hiyaoroshi Sake Is a Seasonal Treasure
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Brewed in winter and matured through summer, hiyaoroshi sake is released only September-November. Ask for 'hiyaoroshi' at sake bars or department stores for this limited seasonal pour.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Street Food Calendar
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Fall/winter: yaki-imo roasted sweet potato (¥300-500, sold from trucks). Summer: kakigori shaved ice. Spring: sakura mochi and ichigo daifuku (strawberry mochi).

seasonalFoodTips.title: Strawberry Picking Season Peak
seasonalFoodTips.summary: March is peak strawberry (ichigo) season. Farms near Tokyo and Osaka offer all-you-can-eat picking for ¥1,500-2,500 for 30 minutes.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Momiji Hunting Best Spots
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Koyo (autumn colors) peaks mid-November in Kyoto, early November in Tokyo, late October in mountains. Nikko and Kyoto are famously spectacular.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Specials — Ask for Shun no Mono
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Ask the chef "shun no mono wa nan desu ka?" (what's in season?) to get the day's best fish. This shows respect for the craft and often unlocks off-menu items.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Kairo Heat Packs
seasonalFoodTips.summary: In winter, buy kairo (カイロ) disposable heat packs at convenience stores. They last 12+ hours. Stick-on versions go inside clothes. Locals swear by them.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Mono no Aware — The Beauty of Impermanence
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Mono no aware (物の哀れ) is the bittersweet awareness that beauty is fleeting. It explains why cherry blossoms move people so deeply — their significance comes precisely from how briefly they bloom.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Early Sakura in Shizuoka and Kawazu
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Kawazu-zakura in Izu Peninsula bloom from late February through mid-March, 3-4 weeks before Somei Yoshino. A day trip from Tokyo on the Odoriko Express.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hotel Prices Rise Late March
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Accommodation prices spike 30-50% from late March through mid-April in cherry blossom hotspots like Kyoto, Tokyo, and Osaka. Book 2-3 months ahead.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Okayama Korakuen Garden
seasonalFoodTips.summary: One of Japan's top 3 landscape gardens. Wide lawns, ponds, plum groves, and views of Okayama Castle. ¥410 entry. Best in plum blossom season (Feb-March) or autumn. Allow 90 minutes.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Experience Sumida River Fireworks
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Plan your summer visit to Tokyo to coincide with the spectacular Sumida River Fireworks Festival, usually held in late July.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Plum Blossoms Peak in Early March
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Ume (plum) blossoms peak in early-to-mid March before cherry blossoms. Kitano Tenmangu in Kyoto and Kairakuen in Mito are top spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: University Graduation Season
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Late March is graduation season. Students in hakama (formal kimono) crowd photo spots and restaurants. Book reservations early for popular dinner spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Fall Foliage on Mount Rokko
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Mount Rokko in late fall (late October to mid-November) for stunning autumn leaf viewing across its botanical gardens and hiking trails.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Late Sakura: Northern Japan Through Late April
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Cherry blossoms reach Sendai around April 10, Kakunodate (Akita) mid-April, and Hirosaki Castle (Aomori) around April 20. Hokkaido blooms in May.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Tohoku Cherry Blossoms — 2-3 Weeks After Tokyo
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Cherry blossoms in Tohoku peak 2-3 weeks after Tokyo (mid to late April). Kakunodate's samurai district and Hirosaki Castle's 2,600 trees are Tohoku's top hanami spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Winter Travel in Tohoku
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Tohoku gets heavy snowfall November-March. Mountain areas can accumulate 2-3 meters. Pack insulated waterproof boots, layers, and hand warmers. Trains may be delayed by snow — allow buffer time.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Tohoku's Autumn Foliage
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Tohoku's fall colors peak October (mountains) to early November (valleys) — earlier than Kyoto/Tokyo. Naruko Gorge, Oirase Stream, and Towada-ko are the top koyo spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Experience Kobe Luminarie (Winter)
seasonalFoodTips.summary: If visiting in early December, don't miss the Kobe Luminarie, a spectacular light festival commemorating the Great Hanshin Earthquake.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Wisteria Season: Late April Through Mid-May
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Wisteria (fuji) cascades peak in early-to-mid May. Ashikaga Flower Park (Tochigi) and Kawachi Wisteria Garden (Fukuoka) are world-famous. Book timed tickets.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Food: Takenoko and Spring Menus
seasonalFoodTips.summary: April brings takenoko (bamboo shoots), sakura shrimp, spring cabbage, and tai (sea bream). Restaurants launch special spring kaiseki courses and bento sets.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Spring Festival Season
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Takayama Spring Festival (Apr 14-15), Kanamara Matsuri in Kawasaki (first Sunday), and Miyako Odori geisha dances in Kyoto (all April) define the festival calendar.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Tsuyu Ends Late July
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Rainy season typically ends in Kanto/Kansai around July 19-21. The transition to full summer is abrupt: expect 30-33°C and high humidity within days.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Pack for Cold, Dry Winter Air
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Bring warm layers, including thermal underwear, waterproof outer shell, gloves, scarves, and warm hats. February can be very cold and dry, especially in northern regions.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Typhoon Season Delays
seasonalFoodTips.summary: September-October typhoons can halt trains for hours. Check weather forecasts and have backup plans. JR East app shows real-time disruptions.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Discover Minoo Park's Fall Leaves
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Minoo Park during fall to witness stunning autumn foliage and enjoy a scenic hike to Minoo Waterfall, accessible by train from Umeda.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Peak Summer Heat: 33-38°C Daily
seasonalFoodTips.summary: August is Japan's hottest month. Tokyo and Osaka regularly hit 35°C+ with oppressive humidity. Limit outdoor exposure between 11am-3pm.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Eats: Katsuo and Spring Vegetables
seasonalFoodTips.summary: May brings hatsu-gatsuo (first bonito), sansai (mountain vegetables), and sora-mame (fava beans). Izakaya and kaiseki menus showcase these spring ingredients.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hokkaido Escapes the Rain
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Hokkaido has no tsuyu season. June offers 20-25°C weather, lavender fields starting to bloom, and none of the humidity that blankets Honshu.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Dining: Ayu and Cold Noodles
seasonalFoodTips.summary: June introduces ayu (sweetfish) grilled on sticks at riverside restaurants, hiyashi chuka (cold ramen), and zaru soba (chilled buckwheat noodles).

seasonalFoodTips.title: Summer Evening Events: Beer Gardens and Rooftops
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Department store rooftop beer gardens open June-September. All-you-can-drink plans run ¥3,500-5,000 for 2 hours with basic food included.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Yukata Season at Summer Festivals
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Summer festivals are the occasion to wear yukata (cotton kimono). Rent one for ¥3,000-5,000 or buy at Uniqlo/Shimamura from ¥4,000. Staff will help you dress.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Fall Foliage Progression: North to South
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Koyo (autumn color) peaks in Hokkaido early October, Tohoku mid-October, Kanto/Chubu late October, and Kansai/western Japan in November.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Shaved Ice: Japan's Summer Essential
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Kakigori (shaved ice) appears at every festival, cafe, and convenience store. Premium kakigori from specialty shops in Tokyo and Kyoto costs ¥800-1,500 with real fruit syrups.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Fall Foliage Routes
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Trains to Nikko, Hakone, and Kyoto get crowded during peak koyo (Nov). The Sagano Romantic Train and Hakone Tozan Railway offer scenic autumn views.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Typhoon Awareness Begins
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Typhoon season ramps up in August. Track storms on weather apps 3-5 days out. Flights and ferries cancel; shinkansen may delay. Have flexible backup plans.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Verify Winter Operating Hours
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Some outdoor attractions, gardens, or smaller museums may have reduced operating hours or closures during the colder, darker days of January. Always check ahead online.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Heat Lingers Through Mid-September
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Early September still hits 30-33°C in Tokyo and Osaka. The summer heat doesn't truly break until the autumn equinox around September 22-23.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Early Fall Foliage in Hokkaido
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Daisetsuzan National Park shows Japan's first autumn colors from mid-September. The mountaintop koyo at Asahidake and Kurodake peaks around September 20-25.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Typhoon Season Peaks in September
seasonalFoodTips.summary: September is Japan's most active typhoon month. 2-3 typhoons typically approach or make landfall. Monitor forecasts daily and keep plans flexible.

seasonalFoodTips.title: September Hotel Deals Continue
seasonalFoodTips.summary: September remains low season for inbound tourism. Hotel prices stay 20-30% below peak across most cities. Typhoon risk keeps crowds down.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Okinawa Still in Summer Mode
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Okinawa stays 28-31°C through September with warm ocean water (27°C). Fewer tourists than July-August but typhoon risk is highest. Great diving conditions between storms.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Transition: Sanma and Matsutake Arrive
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Sanma (Pacific saury) grilled whole with daikon is September's iconic dish. Matsutake mushrooms appear at high-end restaurants from ¥3,000 for a small course.

seasonalFoodTips.title: December Hotel Pricing: Mixed Bag
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Early-to-mid December offers good hotel value (low season). Prices spike December 23-January 3. Christmas Eve in Tokyo is especially expensive at upscale hotels.

seasonalFoodTips.title: December Weather: Cold but Clear
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Tokyo/Osaka: 5-12°C daytime, near freezing at night. Kyoto drops to 2-8°C. Hokkaido is fully snowy (-5 to 2°C). Pack proper winter gear.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Osechi and Year-End Food Culture
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Osechi-ryori (New Year's food boxes) appear at department stores from mid-December. Toshikoshi soba on Dec 31 symbolizes longevity. Year-end markets sell seasonal ingredients.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Ski Season Opens in Hokkaido and Nagano
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Niseko, Furano, and Hakuba open late November-early December. Hokkaido gets Japan's best powder (Japow). Lift tickets ¥5,000-7,000/day.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Photography Golden Hour Shifts Earlier
seasonalFoodTips.summary: October sunset moves to 5:00-5:30pm. Golden hour at temples starts around 4pm. Plan your best photo spots for late afternoon rather than the 6:30pm summer window.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Autumn Kaiseki: Seasonal Menu Peak
seasonalFoodTips.summary: October-November kaiseki menus feature matsutake mushroom, chestnut (kuri), persimmon (kaki), and sweet potato. This is the culinary high season.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Christmas Illuminations Start Mid-November
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Winter illuminations launch in late November. Kobe Luminarie, Tokyo Midtown, Marunouchi, and Roppongi Hills light up weeks before Christmas.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Seasonal Food: Oden and Nabe Season Begins
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Oden (simmered fish cake stew) appears at every konbini from November. Nabe (hot pot) restaurants launch seasonal menus. Warm, filling, and affordable from ¥500.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Early Spring Plum Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Fukuoka in late winter to early spring (February-March) to admire beautiful plum blossoms (ume), particularly at Dazaifu Tenmangu Shrine.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Experience Hakodate's Magical Winter Illumination
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit from December to February to witness Hakodate's streets, particularly around the Red Brick Warehouses, adorned with beautiful winter illuminations.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Cherry Blossoms at Peace Park
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Experience the serene beauty of cherry blossoms blooming around Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park and the Motoyasu River in early spring.

seasonalFoodTips.title: 2026 Cherry Blossoms Are Predicted Earlier Than Average
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Tokyo: peak ~March 28. Kyoto/Osaka: peak ~March 31. Sapporo: peak ~April 27. Dates shift 5-7 days based on winter temperatures. Follow JMC forecasts starting January.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Asukayama Park — Tokyo's Uncrowded Cherry Blossom Alternative
seasonalFoodTips.summary: On Tokyo's north side, this park has hundreds of cherry trees, a fraction of Ueno Park's crowds, a small monorail, and children's play areas. Perfect for families.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Matsuyama Castle Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Matsuyama Castle in spring to witness breathtaking cherry blossoms adorning the castle grounds and surrounding park.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Kyoto's Autumn Foliage Is Peaking Later Due to Climate Shifts
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Climate change is pushing Kyoto's peak maple colors into mid-December in warm years. Plan later dates than traditional guides suggest. 2025 maples peaked around December 15.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Dress Warm for Sapporo Winters
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Sapporo winters are severely cold and snowy; pack heavy winter clothing, including waterproof jackets, thermal layers, hats, gloves, and sturdy, non-slip boots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Autumn Colors Move South — Opposite to Cherry Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Hokkaido peaks late September-October, Nikko late October, Tokyo/Kyoto late November-December. Maple peaks about a week before ginkgo in most locations.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Cherry Blossoms at Tsurumai Park
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Tsurumai Park in late March to early April to experience one of Nagoya's most beautiful cherry blossom (sakura) viewing spots.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hokkaido Skips Rainy Season Entirely
seasonalFoodTips.summary: While the rest of Japan endures tsuyu from early June to mid-July, Hokkaido has significantly less rain and lower humidity. It's the best June destination in Japan.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Yokohama: Noge Retro Bars and Harbor Views
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Noge (野毛) near Sakuragichō Station is a retro bar district with 600+ tiny bars, tachinomi, and jazz clubs. Minato Mirai's rooftop bars offer harbor night views with cocktails from ¥800.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Okinawa's Rainy Season Starts and Ends a Month Earlier
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Okinawa enters tsuyu in mid-May and exits by late June — a full month before the mainland. Visit Okinawa in July for post-rain beach weather before typhoon season peaks.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Tsuyu Is Not Constant Rain — Plan Around It
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Rainy season means intermittent rain, not continuous downpour. Waterproof shoes are the most important item. Many temples and gardens are more beautiful in the mist.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Photograph Jozenji-dori Trees
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Capture the iconic zelkova trees lining Jozenji-dori Avenue, which provide beautiful greenery in spring/summer and vibrant fall foliage. The street is perfect for scenic strolls.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Enjoy Ritsurin Garden's Fall Colors
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Ritsurin Garden in late fall (mid-November to early December) to witness stunning autumn foliage, especially around its ponds and maple trees.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Kanazawa: Snow-Covered Kenrokuen Lanterns
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Kenrokuen's Kotoji Lantern dusted in snow is an iconic winter image. After fresh snowfall, arrive at 7am opening. The garden hosts special winter illumination events in February.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Sendai: Zuihoden in Autumn Foliage
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Zuihoden mausoleum's ornate gold-and-black facade framed by red and orange maples peaks mid-November. Morning light (9-10am) hits the front directly. Bring a polarizer to cut glare.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Nara: Deer with Cherry Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Nara Park's deer with falling cherry blossoms (early-mid April) is an iconic spring image. Bring shika senbei (¥200) to lure deer under blooming trees for the shot.

seasonalFoodTips.title: January-February Is the Best Value Season in Japan
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Lowest accommodation prices, fewest crowds, excellent winter seafood (crab, oyster, fugu). Combine with Sapporo Snow Festival, onsen trips, and winter illuminations.

seasonalFoodTips.title: See Sankeien Cherry Blossoms
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Visit Sankeien Garden in spring to witness stunning cherry blossom (sakura) views amidst its traditional Japanese landscape and historic buildings.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Free Mugicha at Restaurants in Summer
seasonalFoodTips.summary: During summer, many restaurants serve free mugicha (麦茶, roasted barley tea) instead of water. It's caffeine-free, slightly nutty, and served ice-cold.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Okinawa Beach Etiquette
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Don't take coral or shells from beaches - it's illegal. Use reef-safe sunscreen. Jellyfish are common in summer - look for warning signs.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Mt. Fuji Climbing Season
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Official climbing season is July-early September only. Book mountain huts months ahead. Start at night to reach summit for sunrise. It's harder than it looks!

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hokkaido Driving in Winter
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Winter roads can be icy and treacherous. Rent cars with studded tires, drive slowly, and check road conditions. Chains may be required in mountains.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Cherry Blossom Packed Trains
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Late March to mid-April: trains to popular hanami spots (Ueno, Meguro, Arashiyama) are extremely crowded on weekends. Go early morning.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Hanami Picnic Etiquette
seasonalFoodTips.summary: During cherry blossom season, don't shake branches, damage trees, or reserve spots overnight. Clean up completely after picnics.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Yozakura Night Viewing
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Many parks illuminate cherry blossoms at night (yozakura). Bring warm clothes - spring nights are cold. Food stalls often set up for evening crowds.

seasonalFoodTips.title: Summer Heat Precautions
seasonalFoodTips.summary: Japanese summers are hot and humid (35°C+). Carry a hand towel, stay hydrated, and use cooling products from convenience stores. Heat stroke is real.
